description: An island in the Sea of Oman is home to the "City of Caves", where an important assemblage of rock-cut art dating to the fifth through eighth centuries and linked to the cult of Shiva may be found.

name: Temple of Asclepius
description: The Temple of Asclepius (VIII, 7, 25) in Pompeii is located at the intersection of the Via del Tempio di Iside and the Via Stabiana. It is also referred to as the Temple of Jupiter Meilichius.

name: Lydney Roman temple
description: A fourth century AD temple dedicated to Nodens.

name: Srir
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 D4 Srir

name: Zeus Madbachos, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 D4 Zeus Madbachos, T.

name: Alto da Vigia
description: A Roman sanctuary located at the mouth of the Colares river.

name: Kerlescan alignments
description: A complex of 555 megaliths arranged in 13 rows.

name: Temple of Mater Matuta at Satricum
description: A sanctuary dedicated to Mater Matuta that was in use between the seventh and fifth centuries B.C.

name: Altar at the temple of Amphiaraos
description: An altar located to the east of the temple of Amphiaraos.

name: Enunmah
description: The ḫilṣu-building of the goddess Ningal inside the Egišnugal temple at Ur. The Sumerian ceremonial name Enunmah means "House of the Exalted Prince."

name: Egipar
description: The Giparu-House at Ur was the temple of the high-priestess of the moon-god.

name: Ebabbar
description: The temple of the god Šamaš at Larsa. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ebabbar means "Shining House."

name: Temple of Apollo Daphnephoros
description: The Temple of Apollo Daphnephoros at Eretria dates originally to the late archaic period.

name: Temple of Apollo at Hierapolis
description: The Temple of Apollo at Hierapolis, built during the Hellenistic period.

name: Ea temple (Khorsabad)
description: As part of the palatial complex of Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad), the eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) constructed six temples, including one to the god Ea.

name: Yalburt Hittite sanctuary
description: A sanctuary dating to the Late Hittite Empire, with a hieroglyphic Luwian inscription commemorating the achievements of Tudhaliya IV (thirteenth century BC).

name: Sanctuary of Isis at Dion
description: The sanctuary of Isis at Dion dates to the second century A.D.

name: Hexapyla
description: A sanctuary located north of Syracuse.

name: Mandra di Mezzo
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 C3 Mandra di Mezzo

name: Marchito
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 E4 Marchito

name: Mollarella
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 D4 Mollarella

name: Monte Poliscia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 D4 Monte Poliscia

name: Olympieion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 G4 Olympieion

name: Pietra Rossa
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 E4 Pietra Rossa

name: Palike
description: An ancient settlement in east central Sicily, atop the Rocchicella, which included a sanctuary to the Divine Palikoi (a pair of boiling lakes) within a grotto found on the hillside.

name: Fanum Carisi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 48 B2 Fanum Carisi

name: Mithraeum of Lucretius Menander
description: The Mithraeum of Lucretius Menander located in ancient Ostia dates to the third century CE.

name: Sanctuary of Heqaib (Elephantine)
description: Middle Kingdom sanctuary of Heqaib located on Elephantine Island near the Temples of Satis and Khnum.

name: Sanctuary of Kalabsha (Elephantine)
description: Ptolemaic sanctuary originally situated in Kalabsha, relocated to the southwestern tip of Elephantine Island in 1961.

name: Temple of Nabû of the ḫarû at Assur
description: Sîn-šarra-iškun (626–612 BC), Assyria’s penultimate king, built a new temple for the god Nabû of the ḫarû at Assur. The temple was constructed in vacant lot that was next to the temple of the Assyrian Ištar. That Neo-Assyrian king modelled the temple’s plan on that the Nabû temple at Kalhu (Nimrud) and named it after the Nabû temple at Babylon. Egidrukalamasumu (“House Which Bestows the Scepter of the Land”) was destroyed in 614 BC, when Assur was destroyed by the Medes.

name: Temple of Athena (Ilium)
description: The Temple of Athena at Ilion was a peripteral temple executed in the Doric order with a 12 x 6 columnar arrangement. Its creation is traditionally ascribed to Lysimachus (ca. 301-281 BCE), though it was later restored under Roman influence, likely during the principate of Augustus. Little of the temple, save its forecourt, remains in situ. It is mostly known from membra disiecta, including metopes uncovered during Schliemann's excavations.

name: Temple of Amon (Karnak)
description: Middle Kingdom temple of Amon at the heart of the Karnak Temple Complex.

name: Temple of Athena Alea at Tegea
description: An ancient sanctuary at Tegea dedicated to Athena Alea. The extant phase of the temple is attributed to Skopas who was responsible for rebuilding following a fire in 394 B.C.

name: Mithraeum of Felicissimus
description: The so-called Mithraeum of Felicissimus is a Mithraic shrine located in ancient Ostia. The shrine itself was installed in a second-century CE building and likely dates to the later third-century CE.

name: Classical Temple at Ai Lia
description: Distyle in antis temple of the fourth century BCE, built from the salvage of the large archaic temple, likely destroyed in the earthquake of 373 BCE. This temple remained in use until the first or second centuries CE.

name: Temple of Jupiter
description: The Temple of Jupiter (VII,8,1) in Pompeii sits at the north end of the forum. It was built in the middle of the second century BC.

name: Clepsydra of the Oropos Amphiareion
description: The Oropos Amphiareion clepsydra is a hydraulic inflow clock serving the Sanctuary of Amphiaraus. Situated on the southeast bank of the sacred spring of Amphiaraus, this inflow hydraulic clock dates to the 4th century BCE.

name: Jandial Temple
description: Jandial is the site of a semi-Classical temple of Ionic Greek design constructed in the first or second centuries BCE. It is the farthest east of any known Hellenic temple.

name: Great Temple of Petra
description: The Great Temple of Petra is one of the major archaeological and architectural components of central Petra. It is located to the south of the Colonnaded Street and southeast of the Temenos Gate and covers 7560 square meters.

name: Temple of the Winged Lions
description: The Temple of the Winged Lions is a colonnaded temple built in Petra in order to honor the Arabian goddess al-Uzza.

name: Arsameia pros Nymphaio
description: An ancient fortified city on high ground on the south bank of the river Nymphaios (modern Kâhta Çayı) south of the modern village of Kocahisar (also known as Eski Kahta) in Turkey.

name: Chordiraza?/Baisampse?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 G3 Chordiraza?/Baisampse?

name: el-Hosn
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 C4 el-Hosn

name: Kalota
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 D4 Kalota

name: Nymphaion/Balaneion Tiberinon
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 B4 Nymphaion/Balaneion Tiberinon

name: Sanctuary of Minerva at Spinera
description: A site sacred to Minerva with activity from the Early Iron Age onward, the Sanctuary of Minerva at Spinera is located at Breno on the banks of the river Oglio.

name: Is Pirois
description: A sacred well sanctuary, Pozzo sacro Is Pirois, is associated with the Nuragic culture. Is Pirois is located in the municipality of Villaputzu, in metropolitan Cagliari. The well is built of schist, and the covering structure is a pseudo-dome or tholos.

name: Mnajdra Megalithic Temple (Malta)
description: Megalithic temple complex of the fourth millennium BC.

name: Altar of the Twelve Gods
description: A rectangular peribolos with an altar in the centre, built in 522/1 BC, destroyed in the Persian Sack of 480/79 BC, and rebuilt in the late 5th century BC. It was the central milestone from which distances in Attica were measured. The remains are now mostly underneath the Athens-Piraeus Electric Railway.

name: Sanctuary of Pankrates
description: A sanctuary located by the Ilissos River, east of Athens, consisting of a rock-cut courtyard, where over 50 votive reliefs were found, dedicated to an older god called Pankrates ("all-powerful"), Palaimon, Plouton ("wealth") and "God", and a younger god called Pankrates and Herakles Pankrates.

name: Sanctuary of Amynos and Asklepios
description: A sanctuary of the healing heroes Amynos and Asklepios, located between the Pnyx and the Areopagos in Athens. Archaeological evidence stretches from the 4th century BC until the Roman Imperial period.

name: Asklepieion of Trikka
description: The Asklepieion of Trikka was said to be the oldest such sanctuary in Greece.

name: Temple of Roma and Augustus (Athens)
description: The Temple or Altar of Roma and Augustus on the Acropolis of Athens was erected shortly after 27 BC near the east front of the Periklean Parthenon.

props.ancientName: ho en Akropolei bōmos tōn Sebastōn

name: Temple of Artemis (Corfu)
description: An Archaic Greek temple of the Doric order built ca. 580 BCE.

name: Sanctuary of Herakles Hippodetos
description: Pausanias describes the Sanctuary of Herakles Hippodetos located in the Teneric plain

name: Torricella
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 45 G4 Torricella

name: Zeus Aglaios (temple)
description: A sanctuary of Zeus Aglaios located west of Metapontum.

name: Apollo Alaeus (temple)
description: A sanctuary of Apollo near Punta Alice. Tradition ascribed its foundation to Philoctetes and it is said he dedicated his bow to Apollo.

name: Temple of Hera Lacinia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 46 F3 Hera, T.

name: Arcia di Niscemi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 E4 Arcia di Niscemi

name: Bitalemi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 E4 Bitalemi

name: Brikinniai?
description: A fortified ancient settlement near modern Lentini. It has been associated with the area of Monte San Basilio.

name: Camarina
description: Camarina was founded by Syracuse in 599 B.C., but subsequently destroyed in 552 B.C. The city was re-founded by Gela in 461 B.C. Carthage razed the site in 405 B.C., but Timoleon restored it in 339 B.C. From the late third century B.C. the town fell under Roman administration.

name: Casalicchio
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 D4 Casalicchio

name: Mithraeum at Segontium
description: A mithraeum was excavated at Segontium (modern Caernarfon) in 1959, ca. 137m from the east corner of the Roman auxiliary fort. The site was abandoned in the late fourth century A.D.

name: Temple of Hercules Victor
description: A monopteros in the Forum Boarium at Rome built in the late second century BC, perhaps by L. Mummius Achaicus.

name: Sacred Area of S. Omobono
description: An archaeological area in the Forum Boarium comprising an ancient ritual precinct with twin temples ranging in date from the Archaic period through the time of the Roman Empire. The Medieval church dedicated to S. Omobono stands atop the remains of one of the two ancient temples.

name: Nabû temple (Khorsabad)
description: The eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) had a temple to the god Nabû, the god of scribes, built in the citadel of his new capital, Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad). Building H is the largest temple in Sargon’s newly-constructed city and it was built on a platform in the western side of the citadel, rather than on the palace terrace. The Nabû temple was connected to the temple district, which formed part of Sargon’s royal residence, by a stone bridge.

name: Sebetti temple (Khorsabad)
description: A temple dedicated to the Sebetti, the divine seven, was excavated by Iraqi archaeologists in 1957. This temple at Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad) is located just west of the citadel, near Citadel Gate A and City Gate 7.

name: Sîn temple (Khorsabad)
description: As part of the palatial complex of Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad), the eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) constructed six temples, including one to the god Sîn.

name: Ninurta temple (Khorsabad)
description: As part of the palatial complex of Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad), the eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) constructed six temples, including one to the god Ninurta.

name: el-Nadura
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 79 D3 el-Nadura

name: [Per-ousekh]
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 79 D3 [Per-ousekh]

name: Apa Phoibammon (monastery)
description: The seventh-century monastery of St. Phoibammon was built on the upper terrace of the temple of Hatshepsut at Deir el-Bahri-

name: Karnak
description: A large temple complex in Egypt, associated with the ancient site of Thebes and Luxor and inscribed together with them on the UNESCO World Heritage list.

name: Kubaniya
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 80 inset Kubaniya

name: Temple of Min and Isis
description: This temple is located at the modern Egyptian village of El-Qala, which is near Coptos (modern Qift). It was built likely under Augustus, expanded by Caligula and Claudius, and survived into the Coptic period.

name: Resras
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 80 B4 Resras

name: Shanhur
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 80 B2 Shanhur

name: Tentyra
description: The Ptolemaic temple to Hathor at Dendera.

name: Brean Down
description: Brean Down is the site of a Romano-British temple that was built ca. A.D. 340 and destroyed about 50 years later. The site was excavated between 1957-8.

name: Brigstock
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 G2 Brigstock

name: Coleshill
description: Excavations in the late 1970s revealed evidence for a multi-phase sanctuary. The Iron Age site was of circular plan, while the Romano-British site, of two phases, consisted of a walled enclosure with two shrines.

name: Daventry
description: Roman building interpreted either as a villa or a shrine at Borough Hill hillfort, United Kingdom.

name: Farley Heath
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 G3 Farley Heath

name: Harlow
description: Harlow is the site of a pre-Roman and Roman sanctuary.

name: Hayling Island Iron-Age/Roman Sanctuary
description: Modern Hayling Island (borough of Havant, Hampshire, England) is the site of a multi-phase sanctuary: the Iron Age shrine dates ca. 50 BCE and the Roman temple dates ca. 55-60CE. The sanctuary seems to have suffered decline and ceased being maintained in the early third century CE, although ritual/votive deposition seems to have continued through the fourth century.

name: Henley Wood
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 E3 Henley Wood

name: Hockwold
description: A Romano-Celtic temple site dating between the second and fourth centuries AD.

name: Jordan Hill Roman Temple
description: A Romano-Celtic temple dating probably to the fourth century CE. Its ancient name is unknown. The name of the hill on which it sits is spelled "Jordon" in some twentieth-century works, including the Barrington Atlas.

name: Dimri
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 81 C2 Dimri

name: Forum Cambusis?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 81 B3 Forum Cambusis?

name: Kobosh
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 81 C2 Kobosh

name: Lydney
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 E3 Lydney

name: Paptoulis?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 81 C2 Paptoulis?

name: Parembole?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 81 C2 Parembole?

name: Soleb
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 81 A5 Soleb

name: Tutzis
description: Toutzis (Dendur) is located in Nubia, approximately 80 km south of modern Aswan. In 23 BCE the Roman provincial governor Petronius built a temple at the direction of Augustus. The temple was dedicated to Isis and the deified Nubian brothers Pedesi and Pihor. Imperiled by the Aswan High Dam project, the temple was relocated by UNESCO in 1963. Egypt gifted the temple to the Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York City, where it has been exhibited since 1978.

name: Tzitzis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 81 C2 Tzitzis

name: Nettleton
description: A significant cult center at Nettleton Shrub in Wiltshire was dedicated to Apollo Cunomaglus.

name: Tara
description: Tara is an archaeological site comprising a number of ancient monuments. Tradition holds that the site was the seat of the High King of Ireland.

name: Vagniacis
description: Vagniacis was a small Roman site of the first to fifth centuries with likely specialized religious functions. A series of five temples are situated within a temenos along the Watling Street at Springhead.

name: Wanborough
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 G3 Wanborough

name: Weycock Hill
description: Weycock Hill is the site of a Roman temple excavated in 1847. According to Lewis, this was an octagonal temple with a portico. It dates to the early fourth century CE with abandonment in the fifth century.

name: Woodeaton
description: A Romano-British temple of two phases dating to the first century.

name: Musawwarat
description: A Meroitic temple complex in modern Sudan, located 180 km northeast of Khartoum, 20 km north of Naqa and approximately 25 km south-east of the Nile. The complex dates to the early Meroitic period (third century B.C.). In 2011 the site was enrolled as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

name: Prummu?/Primii?
description: The one-time capital of the ancient Kerma culture in Nubia.

name: Ruwwafah
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 83 B4 Ruwwafah

name: Temple of Sulis Minerva
description: 1st century CE temple to Minerva at the Aquae Sulis in Bath, England in use roughly until 410 AD.

name: Temple of Sîn and Šamaš at Assur
description: A double-temple dedicated to the moon-god Sîn and the sun-god Šamaš existed at the city Assur from the Old Assyrian period onwards. The plan of this holy structure changed several times over the course of its long history, most notably in the Neo-Assyrian period, in the reign of Ashurbanipal II (883–859 BC). The Sîn temple was called Eḫulḫuldirdirra and the Šamaš temple went by the name Ebabbarra.

name: Temple of Soknebtunis
description: A temple dedicated to a local form of the Egyptian god Sobek, Soknebtunis, at Tebtunis. The temple was built by Ptolemy I in the late fourth-early third centuries and was greatly expanded by Ptolemy XII in the first century BCE.

name: Sanctuary of Artemis Limnatis at Messene
description: A sanctuary on the slopes of Mount Ithome and within the city walls of ancient Messene in the Peloponnese. The sanctuary developed beginning in the 4th-3rd centuries BCE.

name: Mura Abbandonate, Sanctuary of Fondo Decina
description: A terraced sanctuary of the Samnite culture that was in use from the archaic period to the third century B.C.

name: Vacri Italic temple
description: An Italic temple was identified in 1975 in the locality known as Porcareccia, Vacri (Chieti), Italy.

name: Yazılıkaya (Hattusa)
description: A sacred rock-cut sanctuary of the Hittites. The sanctuary was in use from the late sixteenth century B.C. Many of the reliefs date to the reigns of Tudhaliya IV and Suppiluliuma II in the late thirteenth century B.C.

name: Temple of Artemis Orthia
description: An Archaic temple dedicated to Artemis Orthia within the sanctuary of this goddess at Sparta. The site contained rich deposits of both votives and masks.

name: Underground Shrine (Paestum)
description: A small, underground tomb-like structure located in the agora of Paestum, thought to be either a shrine or a heroon.

name: Sanctuary of the Public Lares
description: A sanctuary located on the east side of Pompeii's forum (VII, 9, 3), the Sanctuary of the Public Lares was likely built after AD 62. The sanctuary measures ca. 18 x 21 m and centers on an open central courtyard.

name: Guild Temple and Mithraeum of Fructosus
description: An architectural complex excavated in 1938 at the intersection of Via del Pomerio and Via del Tempio Rotondo in Ostia. The guild temple dates to the reign of Alexander Severus (222 - 235 CE). Based on epigraphic evidence, this shrine may belong to the guild of the stuppatores. A mithraic shrine with a patron named Fructosus is to be found in the substructure of the podium.

name: Temple of Khonsu at Karnak
description: New Kingdom temple to the god Khonsu, constructed by Ramesses III and expanded by Ptolemy III Euergetes.

name: Temple of Artemis Orthasia
description: Remains of a sanctuary located on Mt. Kotilon northwest of the Bassai temple.

name: Mithraeum of the Seven Gates
description: The so-called Mithraeum of the Seven Gates was built at Ostia in the second century CE, repurposing rooms that originally date to the previous century.

name: Temple of Vespasian and Titus
description: A temple in the Roman Forum dedicated to the deified Vespasian that was begun by Titus and completed under Domitian.

name: Temple D
description: The temple referred to as "Temple D" in the Largo Argentina is likely the Aedes Lares Permarini, sacred to the lares who protect sailors. The largest of the temples in this sacred area, "Temple D" dates to the second century B.C. and remains largely unexcavated.

name: Temple A
description: So-called "Temple A" is a structure of the third century B.C. and is likely the Temple of Juturna that was vowed by Caius Lutatius Catulus following his victory against the Carthaginians in 241 B.C. It was later converted into a church, the apse of which remains visible.

name: Temple C
description: The so-called "Temple C" of the Largo Argentina sacred area may be dedicated to the Italic goddess Feronia.

name: So-called twin sanctuary
description: A double sanctuary on the forum of Carsulae. It is no longer clear to which gods these temples belonged.

name: Trapeza/Koumari temple (Achaia)
description: A temple in the Doric order that begins in the sixth century BCE and continues in use until the Hellenistic period. The temple may be associated with ancient Rhypai, at Trapeza near Koumari, Aigio, Achaia.

name: Anahita Temple
description: A platform temple located at Kangāvar in the Kermanshah Province of Iran. Some scholars associate the site with the divinity Anahita.

name: Oracular sanctuary at Oinoe
description: Located to the east of the medieval tower at Oinoe are the remains of a rectangular peristylar building measuring approximately 13.60 x 17.30 meters. The peristyle used marble columns set atop marble pilasters. Spyridon Marinatos discovered the site in 1972.

name: Ancient sanctuary at Marion
description: An Iron Age sanctuary located at Peristeries in Polis Chrysochous, Cyprus, dedicated to the female goddess of Cyprus that was in use for half a millennium, from ca. 950 to ca. 450 BCE.

name: Mithraeum at Fertőrákos
description: A well-preserved Mithraeum with modern stone vault above ground and site below in Fertőrákos, Hungary, at the site of ancient Scarbantia.

name: Poseidon?, T.
description: Poseidon?, T. a sanctuary of Poseidon on the island of Samos.

name: Sinuri, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 F3 Sinuri, T.

name: Thermai
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 E4 Thermai

name: Zeus Melosios, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 B3 Zeus Melosios, T.

name: Agdistis Sanctuary (Yazılıkaya)
description: A plateau around a monumental Phrygian rock‐cut altar at the extensive fortified settlement and sanctuary complex of Yazılıkaya in Turkey. Associated with Agdistis in the 20th century because of Roman epigraphic finds there, more recent excavations have demonstrated that the sanctuary was in use from the 8th–7th century BCE to the Roman Period (1st-2nd century CE). Continuity of ritual practice in front of the rock-cut altar is demonstrated by rock-cut bows, a ritual preparation area with hearths, a rock-cut statue identifiable as the goddess Matar, and the Roman-era votive inscriptions.

description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 E5 Altuncebbar
