description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 A3 Zeus Thimenos, T.

name: Temple of Hadrian (Ephesus)
description: A small, prostyle Roman temple located on Curetes Street in ancient Ephesus, the Temple of Hadrian was dedicated to the emperor Hadrian in 138 CE by Publius Quintilius.

name: Heroon of Trysa
description: The Heroon of Trysa is a scholarly appellation applied to an ancient tomb (ca. 380 BCE) found at Trysa in ancient Lycia. Julius August Schönborn discovered the site in 1841. The shrine is noted for its cycle of relief sculpture.

name: Kalhu ziggurat
description: The temple-tower of the god Ninurta, which was started by the ninth-century-BC Assyrian ruler Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) and completed by his son and successor Shalmaneser III (r. 858–824 BC), is still the most prominent feature of Kalhu (modern Nimrud). This multi-staged ziggurat, whose remains are now a pyramid of eroding mud-brick, was constructed in the northwest corner of the citadel, next to Kalhu's principal sanctuary, the Ninurta temple.

name: Ninurta temple (Kalhu)
description: When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. The most important of those was dedicated to the god Ninurta, the patron deity of city, which Ashurnasirpal had constructed between his palace and the ziggurat, a building also consecrated to Ninurta.

name: Kidmuri temple (Kalhu)
description: When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the divine Kidmuri (probably a collective name for a group of gods).

name: Synagogue at Aḥmadiah (Aḥmadiya, Ahmadiyye)
description: Remains of a synagogue in the Golan with Greek and Hebrew Inscriptions

name: Enamhe
description: The temple of the gods Adad and Apla-Adad at Anat (Anatho). The Sumerian ceremonial name Enamhe means "House of Plenty."

name: Etruscan acropolis at Volaterrae
description: The multi-phase acropolis of Etruscan Volaterrae has numerous important phases, beginning in the Archaic period. The monumental site continued to develop and grow during the Hellenistic period. It was identified archaeologically only in the nineteenth century. In the twentieth century it was the subject of major investigations under M. Cristofani and, later, M. Bonamici.

name: Ehursagtilla
description: The temple of the god Ninurta, Ehursagtilla (“House Which Exterminates the Mountains”), was located in the Šuanna district (modern Ishin Aswad) of Babylon. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, this was one of two temples in that part of the city, which was located in the southern part of the eastern half of Babylon. The building was rebuilt by the Neo-Babylonian king Nabopolassar (r. 625–605 BC).

name: Sanctuary of Eros and Aphrodite
description: A sanctuary of Eros and Aphrodite, located in a fold of the rock on the north slope of the Acropolis of Athens, active from at least the mid-fifth century BC.

name: Skydra Hellenistic Temple
description: A Hellenistic temple, possibly of Zeus, the foundation of which was excavated on the southern outskirts of the residential area of modern Skydra in Greece.

name: Dörpfeld foundations
description: The foundations of a 6th century BC temple on the Acropolis of Athens, dedicated to Athena Polias. It is disputed whether these foundations belong to the same structure as the 6th-century BC architectural fragments known as the Bluebeard temple. At the end of the 6th century, the foundations were reused for another temple of Athena Polias, the Archaios Neos, which was destroyed by the Persians in 480 BC.

name: Great altar of Athena
description: The altar of Athena Polias on the Acropolis of Athens, built in or before the 7th century BC.

props.ancientName: ho bōmòs ho en tê akropólei

name: Emah
description: The temple of the goddess Ninmah/Bēlet-ilī, Emah (“Exalated House”), was located in the Ka-dingirra district (modern Kasr) of Babylon. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, this was one of four temples in that part of the city, which was located in the northern part of the eastern half of Babylon. Emah was rebuilt by the Assyrian king Ashurbanipal (r. 668–ca. 631 BC) and the Babylonian king Nebuchadnezzar II (r. 604–562 BC).

name: Poggio Colla
description: An Etruscan settlement located in the Mugello valley.

name: Temple of Al-Lat at Palmyra
description: A temple sacred to the goddess Al-Lat in Palmyra. The earliest shrine on the site was constructed in the mid-first century BCE, and was greatly expanded during the second century CE. The temple was closed and its altar destroyed during the 380s CE.

name: Akītu of Šarrat-Nippuri
description: Temple in Babylon, not located, but probably not far from Eḫursagtila, for it is mentioned together in the ritual texts of the "Divine Love Lyrics". Šarrat-Nippuri "The Queen of Nippur" was a hypostasis of Ištar. It is difficult to ascertain the precise reason for the existence of an Akītu temple dedicated to this deity in the city of Babylon.

name: Temple of Asclepios
description: The Asklepieion at Lissus, Crete.

name: Souma el Kiata
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F2 Souma el Kiata

name: Souma n'Ikhetteben
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 E2 Souma n'Ikhetteben

name: Beni Guedal
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 C2 Beni Guedal

name: Bir Nesma
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F3 Bir Nesma

name: el-Chatatba
description: A ruined temple built in Cipollino marble located about 10 km south of Sabratha.

name: Garian
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F2 Garian

name: Gasr el-Gezira
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 E2 Gasr el-Gezira

name: Geran
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F2 Geran

name: Giado
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 E3 Giado

name: Henchir Krebita
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 B1 Henchir Krebita

name: Henchir Oum el-Abbas
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 B1 Henchir Oum el-Abbas

name: Henchir Snem
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 C2 Henchir Snem

name: Mezgura
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 D3 Mezgura

name: Msellata
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F2 Msellata

name: Ras el-Haddagia
description: A sanctuary of Ammon was located at Ras el-Haddagia. The rectangular temple (7.05 x 2.95 m) included an altar and a portico.

name: Rogeban
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 E3 Rogeban

name: Sghedeida
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F2 Sghedeida

name: Tigi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 D2 Tigi

name: Tininai
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F3 Tininai

name: Trigh-Tarhuna
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F2 Trigh-Tarhuna

name: Wadi Antar
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F3 Wadi Antar

name: Wadi Lella
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 E3 Wadi Lella

name: Wadi Merteba
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 C2 Wadi Merteba

name: Wadi Migdal
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F3 Wadi Migdal

name: Zaviet el-Mahgiub
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 G2 Zaviet el-Mahgiub

name: Temple F at Selinus
description: Temple F at Selinus is a Doric temple located on the site's so-called "East Hill". Dedicated either to Athena or Dionysus, the temple dates to the middle of the sixth century B.C. The non-canonical ground plan of 6 x 14 columns gives the temple an elongated aspect.

name: Kuntillat 'Ajrud
description: A late ninth to early eighth century BC site located in the northeastern part of the Sinai peninsula, the fortress-like Kuntillat ‘Ajrud may have been a sanctuary. Hebrew and Phoenician inscriptions have been identified at the site.

name: Monte Li Santi
description: A Faliscan sanctuary located on the slopes of Monte Li Santi.

name: Ta' Ħaġrat Temple Complex
description: Ta' Ħaġrat is a megalithic temple complex on the island of Malta (ca. 3600-3000 B.C.), included in the UNESCO World Heritage List.

name: Temple of Athena (Thasos)
description: An archaic temple of Athena on the acropolis of Thasos was dismantled in the 5th century BCE. Planning, procurement of materials, and construction of its replacement seem to have begun shortly thereafter, but were never finished.

name: Cova de les Dones
description: The cave known as Cova de les Dones is located in the Millares municipality, province of Valencia, Spain. This site shows evidence of human activity across a broad period stretching from the Paleolithic to the time of the Roman Empire.

name: Temple of Zeus at Cyrene
description: The Doric Temple of Zeus at Cyrene.

name: Temple of Vesta at Rome
description: The Temple of Vesta, located at the east end of the Forum Romanum.

name: Temple C at Selinus
description: The so-called Temple C at Selinus is a Doric temple that dates to the second half of the sixth century B.C.

name: Lousoi
description: A settlement in Azania, located near modern Kato Lusi, that has been under excavation since the late 19th century.

name: Lykosoura
description: A city of Arcadia and home of the cult of Despoina

name: Melpeia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 58 B3 Melpeia

name: Menelaion
description: An ancient Greek sanctuary of Helen and Menelaos near ancient Therapnai in Greece.

name: Mysia (settlement)
description: An ancient settlement in the Argolid, mentioned by Pausanias, who highlights there a sanctuary of Demeter. This settlement is probably to be identified with the modern Phyktia.

name: Nemea
description: Nemea, located north of Argos, was the site of the Panhellenic Games and the Sanctuary of Nemean Zeus.

name: Olympia
description: Ancient sanctuary of Zeus and home of the ancient Olympic games.

name: Pera Milous
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 58 G2 Pera Milous

name: Treis Ammoundas
description: A sanctuary in NW Kea located at Treis Ammoundas dating from the Archaic to Hellenistic periods.

name: Zeus, T.
description: The temple of Zeus Ikmaios on Keos.

name: Sanctuary of Zeus Lykaios
description: The sanctuary of Zeus Lykaios on Mt. Lykaion.

name: Temple of Aphaia on Aegina
description: An ancient sanctuary on the island of Aegina, dedicated to the autochthonous deity Aphaia.

name: Aphrodite sanctuary at Dafni
description: An ancient sanctuary of the goddess Aphrodite, described by Pausanias and located at modern Dafni (Daphne) along the ancient sacred way from Athens to Eleusis.

name: Temple of Apollo Zoster
description: The Temple of Apollo Zoster is a sanctuary of the god Apollo located in Vouliagmeni in Attica, Greece. The temple's foundations date to the sixth century BC.

name: Artemis Tauropolos temple
description: Near Artemida/Loutsa in modern Attiki, the archaeological remains of a temple in the Doric order were first identified in 1956. Excavators identified the sanctuary as belonging to Artemis Tauropolos. The compilers of the Barrington Atlas, in keeping with the discussion offered in PECS by C.W.J. Eliot, associate the temple site with the deme of Halai Araphenides.

name: Brauron/sanctuary of Artemis Brauronia
description: Brauron was a sacred site in Attica dedicated to Artemis Brauronia. The sanctuary was active from the Middle Bronze Age period, although a gap in activity stretches from the Final Bronze Age to the eighth century B.C.

name: Temple of Athena at Sounion
description: The Temple of Athena at Sounion (ca. 470 B.C.) with its L-shaped colonnade was built on the site of an earlier temple (sixth century B.C.) at the same location.

name: Temple of Athena Skiras on Salamis
description: This sanctuary, reported by Herodotus, is thought to have been located on the modern Cape Arapis in the northeast part of the island of Salamis, just north of the modern naval base. It is not to be confused with the Temple of Athena Skiras in Phaleron, reported by Pausanias.

name: Demeter Sanctuary (Eleusis)
description: The Sanctuary of Demeter at Eleusis in Attica was the site of the celebration of the Eleusinian mysteries, observed from the Archaic period to Late Antiquity.

name: Herakleion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 59 C2 Herakleion

name: Temple of Nemesis
description: A Doric peripteral temple was built in the sanctuary in the middle of the fifth century BCE. Construction began around 460-450 BCE and continued until 430–420 BCE.

name: Cave of Pan and the Nymphs
description: A small cave, located northeast of Vari in Attica, Greece, that was used as a shrine dedicated to Apollo, Pan, and the Nymphs from the sixth to the second centuries BC.

name: Temple of Poseidon at Sounion
description: The hexastyle temple of Poseidon at Sounion was built ca. 444-440 B.C. atop the ruins of an Archaic period temple.

props.ancientName: Temple of Poseidon at Cape Sounion

name: Zeus Ombrios, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 59 B2 Zeus Ombrios, T.

name: Zeus Ombrios, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 59 C3 Zeus Ombrios, T.

name: Zeus Panhellenios sanctuary (Aegina)
description: An oracular mountaintop sanctuary of Zeus on the island of Aegina.

name: Ardettos
description: A site to the southeast of Athens, where jurors swore their oath, named for a hero Ardettes.

name: Sanctuary of Aphrodite Paphia
description: A major sanctuary to Aphrodite on Cyprus; activity is found as early as the third millennium BCE.

name: Sobek temple, Qaşr Qārūn
description: A Ptolemaic temple to the god Sobek.

name: Temple of Apollo at Metropolis
description: A peripteral Doric temple built in the mid-6th century BCE was destroyed by fire in the 2nd century BCE. The temple faces east, and was built of mudbrick on foundations of local sandstone, with sandstone column drums and a terracotta roof. A 4th-century BCE dedicatory inscription found in the cella attests the worship of Apollo. The structure was discovered in 1994 following the activity of looters.

name: Temple of Jupiter, Damascus
description: The Temple of Jupiter in Damascus was begun during the rule of Augustus and completed during the rule of Constantius II.

name: The Great Altar at Pergamon
description: A monumental altar built during the reign of king Eumenes II.

name: Temple of Trajan at Pergamon
description: The Temple of Trajan at Pergamon, also referred to as the Traianeum, was dedicated to Trajan and Zeus Philios and completed during the reign of Hadrian.

name: Capitolium of Brixia
description: The Capitolium of Brixia was built in A.D. 73.

name: Eremo di San Michele (Coli)
description: The cavern of San Michele was a hermit's dwelling place that is located near a cavern known as the "cave of San Colombano". This hermitage lies in a relatively solitary area of the valley of the Curiasca di San Michele stream, a tributary of the Trebbia river (Italian municipality of Coli, province of Piacenza).

name: Sanctuary of Apollo Phanaios
description: A partially excavated artificial platform and temple at modern Kato Phana on the Greek island of Chios (ancient Phanai).

name: Temple of Trajan at Italica
description: A sanctuary dedicated to Trajan in the 'urbs nova' of Italica that flourished under the patronage of Hadrian.

name: Doric temple of Segesta
description: The Doric temple of Segesta is an incomplete (6 x 14) temple of the Doric order begun in the 420s B.C.

name: Kothon
description: The so-called "Kothon" is a large, rectangular, artificial pool dating to the Punic period of Sicily and located on the island of Motya. Originally interpreted as an artificial port basin, recent research reinterpets it as a freshwater basin with functions sacred to the god Ba'al and constituting the central component of a large sanctuary.

name: Sanctuary of Kalliste
description: A sanctuary for Kalliste ("most beautiful lady"), sometimes identified with Artemis, located on the road from Athens to the Academy.

name: Sanctuary at Lucus Feroniae
description: The sanctuary and grove of Feronia located at the site of Lucus Feroniae.

name: Temple of Poseidon at Isthmia
description: The Poseidon sanctuary at Isthmia lies 16 km to the east of Corinth. The initial phase belongs to the seventh century BC, although the sanctuary was destroyed ca. 470 BC and rebuilt in 440 B.C.

name: Shunet ez Zebib
description: A large mudbrick structure located at Abydos in Upper Egypt that dates to the Second Dynasty (ca. 2700 B.C.) of ancient Egypt.

name: Osireion
description: The Osireion at Abydos is located to the rear of the temple of Seti I. It is an integral part of Seti I's funeral complex and was built to resemble an 18th Dynasty Valley of the Kings tomb.

name: Cybele sanctuary
description: A temple sacred to Cybele located at Neuss am Rhein.

name: Temple of Demeter
description: A Doric temple ascribed to Demeter with origins in the fourth century B.C.

name: Chrysas, T.
description: According to Cicero, the shrine of Chrysas (the god shares a name with the nearby river) was located along the road from Assorus to Henna. A cult image in marble, described as being "beautiful", was to be found there and was ultimately spared from the depredations of Caius Verres.

name: Feudo Nobile
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 E4 Feudo Nobile

name: Fiume di Mallo
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 E4 Fiume di Mallo

name: Fontana Calda di Butera
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 E4 Fontana Calda di Butera

name: Francavilla di Sicilia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 G3 Francavilla di Sicilia

name: Grattauli
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 C3 Grattauli

name: Temple of the Oxus
description: The Temple of the Oxus is a Seleucid-era temple situated within the citadel of Takhti-Sangin, a Graeco-Bactrian town between the Vakhsh and Panj rivers.

name: Temple of Artemis at Sardis
description: A large temple dedicated to Artemis at the city of Sardis in western Asia Minor. A sanctuary to Artemis was constructed on the site during the Persian Era (ca. 547-334 BCE), and construction on a new temple on the site began during the third century BCE. The temple was never fully completed. A small church was built on part of the site in the 5th century CE, but the structure declined from the 7th century onwards, and many of its stones were reused or burned.

name: Mithraeum III at Poetovio
description: The third of five shrines sacred to Mithras found near the site of Poetovio.

name: Halberg Mithraeum
description: A natural cave in the side of the Halberg hill at Saarbrücken was enlarged for the use of the Mithras cult.

name: Sanctuary of Phrontis(?)
description: The site of a shrine assumed to honor to the hero Phrontis.

name: Southeast temple of the Athenian Agora
description: The modern name for a temple built in the south-eastern corner of the Athenian Agora next to the Panathenaic Way in the early second century AD, using material from the Temple of Athena at Sounion. A colossal cult statue of a female deity was located inside. It was demolished in the late third century AD and used as building material for the Post-Herulian Wall.

name: Temple of Athena Pallenis
description: The Temple of Athena Pallenis was built in the mid-6th century BC and rebuilt on a larger scale in the late 5th century BC. In myth, the location was the site of King Eurystheus' tomb and Theseus' victory over the Pallantidai. In 546 BC, it was the site of the battle by which Peisistratos finally established his tyranny. At the end of the 1st century BC, the structure was disassembled and rebuilt in the Agora of Athens as the Temple of Ares.

name: Vaiṣṇava Temple at Udayagiri
description: Temple constructed near the Udayagiri cave complex during the Gupta period (ca. 4th-5th century CE).

name: Aedes Serapis
description: Caracalla dedicated a temple to Serapis in Regio VI. Its dedication is confirmed via epigraphic elements and some of its architectural elements are also known. It was located on the Collis Latiaris, which is the southern most part of the Quirinal Hill.

name: Aedes Salus
description: While consul in 311 BCE, Caius Iunius Bubulcus Brutus vowed the temple of Salus; he would later dedicate it as dictator in 302 BCE. While no physical remains of the temple are known, textual and epigraphic testimony suggest that it would have been located near the present-day Piazza del Quirinale.

name: Karthaia, Athena Temple
description: A Doric-order temple, hexastyle in antis (23.20x11.10 m). The building utilizes stone from both Aegina and Paros.

name: Scargill Moors Roman shrines
description: A complex of two Roman shrines to the god Vintonus on Scargill Moor, near Bowes in County Durham.

name: So-called Area X in Berenike
description: The ruins of a small building (possibly first century AD) with a shrine, in the archaeological site of Sidi Khiribech, in Berenike. According to some archaeologists, it might have been connected to the adjacent Area A, and constituted together a single unit. In other interpretations, it is an independent sacred space.

name: Temple of Ba'al at Palmyra
description: Temple of Ba'al (or Bel) in the ancient city of Palmyra.

name: Irru
description: A nuragic shrine with a sacred well.

name: Fountain of Anna Perenna
description: Sacred to the goddess Anna Perenna and the nymphs, this rectangular fountain surrounded by rupestral inscriptions was excavated on the modern Via Guidubaldo del Monte in Rome's Pinciano neighborhood during the late 20th century. Rich votive deposits complement the picture of the goddess' worship painted by Ovid in the Fasti entry for the Ides of March.

name: Madhukeshwara temple
description: A Shiva temple at Banavasi in India whose foundation may date as early as the 7th century CE.

name: Temple of Zeus at Lebadea
description: The foundations of a temple sacred to Zeus at Lebadea. Pausanias reports that the temple was incomplete, owing perhaps to its over-ambitious size or to the nagging costs associated with warfare.

name: Parco archeologico Griso-Laboccetta
description: An Archaic to Hellenistic sanctuary sacred to Demeter.

name: Santa Maria Sopra Minerva
description: A first century B.C. temple dedicated to Minerva by Gneus Caestius and Titus Caesius Priscus, who were two of the Assisi's quattuorviri. The temple was converted to the church of Santa Maria Sopra Minerva in 1539.

name: Gallo-Roman temple at Vannes
description: Remains of a Gallo-Roman at Vannes.

name: Temple of Minerva at Theveste
description: The Temple of Minerva at Theveste dates to the third century A.D.

name: Capitolium at Mactaris
description: The Capitolium temple at Mactaris likely dates to the reign of Marcus Aurelius.

name: Luxor Temple
description: An ancient Egyptian temple that was initially built ca. 1400 BCE.

name: Gola di S. Andrea, Itri
description: Located along the course of the Via Appia between Fondi and Itri are the remains of a likely sanctuary of Apollo that dates to the fourth and third century BCE.

name: Temple of Dedwen
description: An ancient Egyptian temple that was dedicated to Dedwen, the Nubian serpent goddess. It was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1979, as part of "Nubian Monuments from Abu Simbel to Philae". It was relocated to its current location.

name: Shrine of Sabazius
description: The Shrine of Sabazius was installed in part of a horreum (V, XII, 2) that was initially built ca. 120-125 CE. The Sabazius shrine likely dates to the early third century. Dante Vaglieri excavated the shrine in 1909.

name: Los Cuadriales
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 D3 Los Cuadriales

name: Cueva del Agua
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 D3 Cueva del Agua

name: Foz de Colares
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 A3 Foz de Colares

name: Herakleion
description: An ancient sanctuary of Hercules (Melqart) that Strabo places at one end of the long island (Cotinussa) that extended south from ancient Gades. Significant landscape and coastal change have occurred since antiquity, disrupting the integrity of these islands. Nonetheless, modern scholars have consistently identified the location of the sanctuary as at or near the 17th-18th century Castillio de Sancti Petri, which today occupies a separate homonymous islet. Archaeological finds possibly associated with the sanctuary have been made in the vicinity (both terrestrial and underwater) over the years, but precise location of foundations or other identifiable architecture have remained elusive.

name: Menestheus, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 D5 Menestheus, T.

name: Phosphoros/Lux Dubia (sanctuary)
description: Strabo describes this sanctuary in his narrative of sailing up the Guadalquivir River. His text mentions a sanctuary of Phosphorus - this is likely Diana - that is known as "Lux Dubia" (Strabo transliterates "Lucem dubiam" to Greek characters, thus λοῦκεμ δουβίαν).

name: São Miguel de Mota
description: Site of a temple dedicated to the Celtiberian/Lusitanian god Endovelicus.

name: Cerro de los Santos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 27 D3 Cerro de los Santos

name: Temple 200 at Naga
description: Temple structure located south of the main Amun Temple complex at Naga, constructed during the reign of King Nebmaatre Amanakhareqerema (1st century CE).

name: Arthur's O'On
description: A circular Roman temple that stood on the river Carron until it was destroyed in 1743.

name: Temple of Portunus
description: A tetrastyle temple dedicated to the god Portunus and dating to the late second century or early first century BC.

name: Pānṛ I
description: Buddhist temple complex in Swat, Pakistan occupied from the 1st century CE until the end of the 4th-5th centuries CE.

name: Pattadakal
description: A group of temples featuring nine Hindu temples and a Jain sanctuary dating to the seventh and eighth centuries CE and connected with the Chalukya dynasty.

name: Belvedere Temple, Orvieto
description: A fifth century B.C. temple that was likely sacred to Tinia.

name: Temple of Hibis
description: A large temple located in the Kharga Oasis that is dated to the Saite-Persian period.

name: Fanum Pithiviers-le-Vieil
description: The Fanum of Pithiviers-le-Vieil.

name: Monte Torre Maggiore
description: An Italic temple complex in Umbria of the first millennium B.C. that includes the so-called "Ara maior".

name: Gallo-Roman temple of Forêt d'Halatte (cne. Ognon, Oise)
description: A Gallo-Roman temple built ca. mid-first century AD that remained in use until the fifth century.

name: Scoglietto
description: A Roman sanctuary in use from the first century B.C. through fifth century A.D.

name: Hapsasieion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 A2 Hapsasieion

name: Hecate, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 B2 Hecate, T.

name: Hieron (Byzantion)
description: A sanctuary that was sacred to Poseidon and Zeus Ourios located at the Black Sea mouth.

name: Hieron (Chalkedonion)/Zeus Ourios, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 B2 Hieron (Chalkedonion)/Zeus Ourios, T.

name: Iuppiter Urius, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 B2 Iuppiter Urius, T.

name: Actium
description: Site of an ancient temple of Apollo near which Octavian erected a monument to commemorate his naval victory over M. Antonius in 31 BCE.

name: Ag. Ioannis Rodakis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 C4 Ag. Ioannis Rodakis

name: Apollo Leukatas, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 C4 Apollo Leukatas, T.

name: Idomene Minor
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 D3 Idomene Minor

name: Kalogeros
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 inset Kalogeros

name: Khortata
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 C4 Khortata

name: Magoula
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 C4 Magoula

name: Metropolis (sanctuary)
description: A classical sanctuary, located somewhere near Olpai.

name: Nekromanteion
description: An ancient sanctuary of necromancy sacred to Hades and Persephone located on the banks of the Acheron River in Epirus, near the ancient city of Ephyra.

name: Pecë
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 B2 Pecë

name: Profitis Elias
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 C4 Profitis Elias

name: Roda
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 A2 Roda

name: Spelaion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 C4 Spelaion

name: Zeus Ainesios (temple)
description: A temple of Zeus Ainesios located atop mount Ainos on the island of Cephallania.

name: Ramappa Temple
description: A thirteenth-century temple that was built during the period of the Kakatiya ruler Ganapati Deva.

name: Capitolium of Sarsina
description: The remains of a first-century BCE temple podium discovered at Sarsina have been identified as the Roman settlement's Capitolium.

name: Manganello Sanctuary
description: The Manganello Sanctuary is located at the northern edge Caere's urban plateau.

name: Largo di Torre Argentina
description: A square in the Campus Martius of Rome, at the center of which lies an archaeological site that includes the remains of four temples of the Republican period, as well as elements of the first century B.C. theater complex of Cnaeus Pompeius.

name: Tall Bāzy
description: Archaeological site on the eastern bank of the Euphrates in northern Syria. The site contains a town dated to the Early Bronze Age III-IVA (mid-3rd millennium BCE), as well as a Roman sanctuary used only briefly from about the late 1st-late 2nd century CE. The western part of the site was flooded by the construction of the Tischrin Dam in 1999. Tall Bazi and the nearby site of Banat have been tentatively identified as the city of Armi/Armium mentioned in texts from Ebla.

props.ancientName: Tall Bāzy, Tall Bazy, Tall Bazi

name: Roman temples at Chieti
description: Structures thought to be temples set within a sacred area that includes a sacred well.

name: Capitolium at Thugga
description: The Capitolium at Thugga dates to the middle of the second century and carries epigraphic dedications to Lucius Verus and Marcus Aurelius dating to the late 160s.

name: Rock-cut Sanctuary of Letoids and Nymphs
description: The Elmalı Museum's 2011 discovery of a rock-cut relief and many broken stelai near the fountain known as Sarı Mehmet Çeşmesi, in the Yukarıovacık Yayla (plateau), on the western slopes of Temrencik Tepe, on the border between the territory of ancient Choma and Oinoanda, points to the existence of a spring sanctuary of Leto, her children Apollo and Artemis (the Letoids), and perhaps also 9 nymphs.

name: Temple of Concord
description: The Temple of Concord at Rome was located at the western margin of the Forum Romanum. Its origins are sometimes linked to a vow made by Marcus Furius Camillus in 367 BCE, but the vow may not have been fulfilled until 218 BCE under Lucius Manlius. The sanctuary was rebuilt in 121 BCE and again in 10 CE.

name: Ayn-Manawir Temple
description: Temple dedicated to Osiris located 3km west of the modern village of Dush.

name: Funerary temple of Seti I
description: Temple dedicated to the cult of King Seti I located on the west bank of Thebes.

name: Lacus Iuturnae
description: The Lacus Iuturnae was a pool built near a spring sacred to Iuturna. Legend held that the Dioscuri watered their horses there after the Battle of Lake Regillus in 495 BCE.

name: Qasr el-Sagha temple
description: Temple located north of Lake Qarun in the Fayyum Depression. Tentatively dated to the Old Kingdom period, the temple may have been dedicated to the god Sobek.

name: Temple of Kalabsha
description: An ancient temple dated ca. 30 BCE was originally located at Bab al-Kalabsha (Gate of Kalabsha), some 50 km south of Aswan. It was relocated to its present site during the construction of the Aswan High Dam with sponsorship from the government of Germany.

name: Temple of Amada
description: The Temple of Amada is the oldest Egyptian temple known in Nubia. Founded by Pharaoh Thutmose III (18th dynasty), the temple was dedicated to Amun and Re-Horakhty. The decorative program is attributed to Amenhotep II, his son and successor. The temple was relocated to its current position in 1964 along with the Temple of Derr.

name: Temple of Apollo at Delphi
description: The Temple of Apollo situated in the center of the oracular sanctuary of that god at Delphi. Visible remains today are a 20th century reconstruction of the temple rebuilt in the fourth century BCE following a destructive landslide (repaired and altered multiple times thereafter). The date and cause of the temple's eventual destruction sometime not earlier than late antiquity remain undetermined.

name: Sanctuary of Apollo at Cyrene
description: A sanctuary at Cyrene dedicated to Apollo Karneios. The sanctuary was in use between the seventh century BC and the second century AD, and included shrines to other gods, including Artemis and Isis.

name: Palazzo Barberini Mithraeum
description: The Palazzo Barberini Mithraeum numbers among the best-preserved mithraic shrines in Rome. It is situated in via delle Quattro Fontane. Construction work revealed the site in 1936. A portion of the original structures of the second century CE was transformed into the mithraeum in the third century.

name: *Karistoron
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 49 E1 *Karistoron

name: Eastern sanctuary
description: An extra-mural sanctuary located at the eastern gate of Gabii

name: Tell F6 at Failaka
description: Tell on the southwestern corner of Failaka Island containing the remains of two large buildings, tentatively identified as a palace and a temple, dated to 1900-1400 BCE.

name: Haut Bécherel
description: Haut Bécherel is the site of a major Gallo-Roman sanctuary sacred to Mars.

name: Doric Temple at Kardaki
description: An Archaic Greek temple at Corfu built in the Doric order ca. 500 B.C.

name: Temple of Ptah (Karnak)
description: Originally constructed by the New Kingdom pharoah Thutmose III, this component of the temple complex in Karnak was repaired and embellished multiple times through the Roman period.

name: St. Ulrich
description: An archaeological site first excavated between 1894 and 1897 that has yielded remains of several villas, including a luxury villa of more than 117 rooms, and a sanctuary.

name: Donon
description: A sanctuary of the god Mercury in the Vosges Mountains.

name: Early Mithraeum
description: First phase of a sacred space dedicated to the worship of the god Mithras at Dura-Europos.

name: Middle Mithraeum
description: Second phase of a sacred space dedicated to the worship of the god Mithras at Dura-Europos.

name: Late Mithraeum
description: Final phase of a sacred space dedicated to the worship of the god Mithras at Dura-Europos.

name: Temple in Block X7
description: Small temple located to the northeast inside the military sector at Dura-Europos.

name: Temple of Artemis Nanaia
description: Temple located in block H4 at Dura-Europos.

name: Temple of Zeus Megistos
description: Temple located in block C4 at Dura-Europos.

name: Temple of Zeus Theos
description: Temple located in block B3 at Dura-Europos.

name: Temple of Adonis
description: Temple located in block L5 at Dura-Europos.

name: Temple of Bêl
description: Temple building located on the northwest side of Dura-Europos.

name: Temple of Aphlad
description: Temple building located in the southwest corner of Dura-Europos.

name: Temple of Zeus Kyrios/Baal-Shamin
description: Temple building located on the southwest side of Dura-Europos.

name: Temple of The Gadde
description: The temple at Dura-Europos was named by excavators according to its association with the city's Gadde/Fortune, but it has been more recently recognized as a temple principally devoted to Malakbel.

name: Temple of Atargatis
description: Temple located in block H2 at Dura-Europos.

name: Temple of The Roman Archers
description: Temple situated on a raised podium and accessed by stairs, dedicated to an unknown deity, and located in Block A1 at Dura-Europos.

name: Sanctuary 18/19
description: Small temple building E7-19 with tenemos E7-18 at Dura-Europos.

name: Temple X9
description: Temple of unknown dedication located in Block X9 of Dura-Europos.

name: Military Zeus Temple
description: Small temple with pitched roof presumably dedicated to Zeus, located in the military campus zone Block A1 at Dura-Europos.

name: Sanctuary of Asklepios at Athens
description: A major sanctuary complex located west of the Theatre of Dionysus Eleuthereus on the south slope of the Athenian Acropolis.

name: Preah Ko
description: Temple constructed in the city of Hariharalaya, dedicated in 880 CE by the Khmer king Indravarman I.

name: Crossroads Enclosure
description: A square sacred enclosure in the northwest corner of the Agora, in front of the Stoa Basileios at the meeting point of the west road and the Panathenaic Way. It was built ca. 430 BC around a sacred rock. It was gradually obscured by the rising ground levels and built over in the 3rd century AD.

name: Temple of Dionysos at Eretria
description: The Temple of Dionysos at Eretria dates ca. 350 B.C. It is located in the northwest sector of the city near the so-called West Gate.

name: Italic temple of Castel di Ieri
description: The remains of an Italic temple, in the locality known as "Pié di Francia". The sanctuary dates from the fourth to first centuries B.C.

props.ancientName: Tempio italico di Castel di Ieri

name: Volcanal
description: The Volcanal was an open-air sanctuary of Vulcan located in the valley of the Forum Romanum on the slopes of the Capitoline Hill. The earliest phases of the sanctuary likely predate the forum itself.

name: Aedicula Concordia
description: The Aedicula Concordia was a bronze shrine of Concord located on the Graecostasis in Rome. Cn. Flavius erected it when he was aedile in 304 B.C. It is presumed to have been destroyed when the later Temple of Concord was dedicated in 121 B.C.

name: Temple of Montu
description: Egyptian temple dedicated to the god Montou in Armant, built and enlarged from the Middle Kingdom to the Roman period.

name: Funerary temple of Thutmose IV
description: Temple dedicated to the cult of King Thutmose IV located on the west bank of Thebes.

name: Funerary temple of Siptah and Twosret
description: Temple dedicated to the cult of King Siptah and the Queen Twosret located on the west bank of Thebes.

props.ancientName: Funerary temple of Siptah and Twosret

name: Sanctuary of Ptah and Mertesger
description: Sanctuary dedicated to the god Ptah and the goddess Meretseger, located on the road linking the village of Deir el-Medina to the Valley of the Queens.

name: Temple of Augustales at Centuripae
description: An early Imperial (first to third centuries) temple at Centuripae that has been associated with the priestly college of the Augustales.

name: Temple of Hercules, Lanuvium
description: The Temple of Hercules at Lanuvium.

name: Temple of Jupiter Tonans
description: A small temple to Jupiter the Thunderer, most likely located in the Area Capitolina. Vowed by Augustus in 26 BCE and dedicated in 22 BCE.

name: Southwest Temple in the Agora of Athens
description: A tetrastyle Doric temple in the Athenian agora, built in the Augustan period using material from a Classical stoa at Thorikos and unidentified Hellenistic structures.

name: Nabataean Temple (Wadi Rum)
description: A Nabataean Temple at Wadi Rum

name: Barbar Temples
description: Archaeological site in northern Bahrain associated with the Dilmun culture. Three successive temples were built on the site during the 3rd-2nd millennia BCE.

name: Marmara sanctuary
description: An ancient sanctuary, perhaps sacred to Demeter, that was discovered during tunneling for the Corinth to Patras highway. The terraced site is located at Marmara, SSE of Aigeira. It dates from the seventh century BCE to the third century BCE.

name: Ezida
description: One of the temples in the citadel of Nineveh that was dedicated to Nabû, the god of scribes; it is located north of the temple of the goddess Ištar (Emašmaš). The Sumerian ceremonial name Ezida means "True House."

name: Temple of Montu
description: Egyptian temple dedicated to the god Montou in Medamud, built and enlarged from the Middle Kingdom to the Roman period.

name: Varaha Cave
description: The modern name of a seventh-century Pallava cave temple in Mahābalipuram.

name: Dharmaraja Ratha
description: Modern name of a seventh-century Pallava rock-cut temple in Mahābalipuram.

name: Ampouria Parnassou
description: A cave sanctuary, dedicated to Pan and the Nymphs, located near modern Ag. Marina in Phocis and therefore also near the site of Psilo Kastro (possibly ancient Patronis).

name: Temple of Jupiter Heliopolitanus
description: The Temple of Jupiter Heliopolitanus at Baalbek is considered the largest temple of the Roman world. It served as an oracular shrine.

name: Temple of Bacchus
description: Part of the complex of Roman temples at Baalbek, the Temple of Bacchus is one of the best-preserved temples of the Roman world. It is conventionally dated to the second century CE.

name: Telesterion
description: The Telesterion (ca. 435 - 421 B.C.) was a sacred hall at Eleusis used for the annual initiation ceremonies for the cult of Demeter and Persephone known as the Eleusinian Mysteries.

name: Pranu Muttedu
description: Pranu Muttedu is a pre- and protohistoric archaeological site located near Goni, Sardinia. The complex includes standing stones (menhirs) and prehistoric built tombs (domus de janas). The site dates to the Late Neolithic (3200 – 2800 BC) and Early Chalcolithic (2800 – 2600 BC) periods.

name: Antro delle Sorti
description: The "Cave of the Fates" at Praeneste is a sanctuary at the base of the city's terraced sanctuary of Fortuna. It was said to contain wooden oracular tablets.

name: Hekateion
description: The Hekateion is a monumental temple for the goddess Hekate constructed in Lagina ca. 125 BCE.

name: Mithraeum at Casa del Mitreo in Emerita Augusta
description: The well-preserved remains of a second century Mithraeum at Emerita Augusta (mod. Merida, Spain).

name: City Eleusinion at Athens
description: The City Eleusinion was one of the most important sanctuaries in the religious life of ancient Athens. It was the urban “branch” of the sanctuary of Demeter and Kore at Eleusis in use during the Archaic and Classical periods.

name: So-called "Temple of Augustus"
description: A temple dedicated to Octavian by a Lucius Calpurnius. It replaced a temple of ca. 194 B.C. that had in turn been restored by Sulla.

name: Tabo
description: A Kushite site located on Argo Island in Nubia, the location of a large Amun temple.

name: Temple complex at Kommos
description: Multi-phase temple site in the city of Kommos in southern Crete. Temple A was constructed by the late 11th century CE. A larger structure, Temple B, was built over it in the 9th century CE. The temple complex reached its maximum extent when Temple C was built over the site and its surroundings in the 7th century CE.

name: Capitolium of Sala
description: Tetrastyle Corinthian temple located in the Roman forum at Sala (modern Chellah) dating from the early Hadrianic period (c.120 CE).

name: Temple of Magna Mater
description: The Temple of Magna Mater, also known as the Temple of Cybele, was dedicated near the Clivus Victoriae on the Palatine Hill on 11 April 191 BCE. The original temple was destroyed by fire in 111 BCE. Following another series of destructive fires, Augustus restored the temple, as he records in his Res Gestae Divi Augusti.

name: Aedes Apollinis Palatini
description: The temple sacred to the god Apollo on the Palatine Hill in Rome.

name: Temple of Hadrian
description: The Temple of Hadrian at Rome, also referred to as the Hadrianeum, was a temple located in the Campus Martius dedicated to the deified Hadrian by Antoninus Pius in 145 CE. Some of the temple's substantial remains were incorporated into a 17th-century building designed by architect Carlo Fontana in Rome's Piazza di Pietra.

name: Temple of Bel at Dura-Europos
description: A temple building located on the northwest side of Dura-Europos.

name: Augusteum at Carthago Nova/Col. Urbs Iulia
description: A temple dedicated to the imperial cult during the reign of Augustus.

name: Surviving colonnade of the imperial temple at Mérida
description: The incorrectly so-called "Temple of Diana."

name: Aedes Ceres Liber Liberaque
description: The Temple of Ceres, located on the slope of the Aventine Hill in Rome, was originally dedicated by Sp. Cassius in 494/3 B.C.

props.ancientName: Temple of Ceres Liber and Libera

name: Ras il-Wardija
description: A Punic-Roman sanctuary site located on the western coast of Gozo (Gaulos), Malta.

name: Marino Mithraeum
description: A Mithraic shrine of unusual size discovered at modern Marino, Italy in 1963.

name: Temple of Montu (El-Tod)
description: A temple sacred to Montu located at El-Tod, Egypt. The temple's foundations were laid during the Fifth Dynasty and the sanctuary continued in use under the Ptolemies.

name: Temple of Zeus Karaos
description: The foundations of an ancient temple located to the west of ancient Astakos/Dragomestre in Aitoloakarnania.

name: Temple of Jupiter Optimus Maximus
description: The chief temple of the city of Rome was located on the Capitoline Hill.

name: Altar of Zeus Polieus
description: An altar and sanctuary on the Acropolis of Athens, site of the bouphonia ritual during the Dipolieia festival. The shrine is to be identified with a set of cuttings at the highest point on the Acropolis, northeast of the Parthenon.

name: Auguratorium
description: An augural area on the Palatine Hill mentioned in the Regionary Catalogues for Regio X.

name: Temple of Athena Polias
description: Pausanias describes the Temple of Athena Polias at Erythrai as containing a wooden image of the enthroned goddess.

name: Preclassical naiskos in the Parthenon
description: A small shrine with an altar built before 480 BC on the Acropolis of Athens. The northern colonnade of the Parthenon was built around this shrine, preserving it in situ. It is unknown what cult it housed.

name: Kástron Khóstion
description: A complex of structures dating to the Greek and Roman period, with ceramic evidence of prior occupation of the vicinity, on a spur of Palióvouno, overlooking a valley leading to the bay of Saránda on the Korinthian Gulf. The excavators associate this site with the attested classical city of Khorsiai on the basis of epigraphic evidence recovered at the site.

name: Aedes Fauni
description: A temple dedicated to Faunus located on Tiber Island, the Aedes Fauni was dedicated in 196 BCE by Gnaeus Domitius Ahenobarbus and Gaius Scribonius Libo.

name: Temple of Ares
description: The sanctuary with a temple dedicated to Ares is located south of the Altar of the Twelve Gods in the Athenian Agora. The temple was originally erected at Pallene ca. 430 BC and sacred to Athena Pallenis at Pallene. The structure was moved to the Agora in the early first century.

name: Temple of Artemis and Apollo
description: An ancient sanctuary of Artemis and Apollo associated with the ancient settlement of Abai and located near the modern Greek town of Kallipodi.

name: Tempio della Tosse
description: The so-called "Tempio della Tosse" is an ancient Roman building along the river Anio. Its dating is problematic, but it is likely a building of late antiquity that is built on the site of an earlier villa. It has been interpreted as a nymphaeum, a temple, and also as a tomb.

name: Temple of the Sibyl
description: A second-century B.C. Roman temple located on the acropolis of ancient Tibur. The so-called Temple of the Sibyl is a pseudo-peripteral building executed in the Ionic order. Some portions of the structure, including the side walls and rea wall, survive to the height of the capitals.

name: Vaugrenier
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 16 D2 Vaugrenier

name: Aix-en-Diois
description: Among various features at Aix-en-Diois are the remains of baths that could either pertain to a sanctuary or a villa.

name: Allan
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D5 Allan

name: Allonzier-la-Caille
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 G2 Allonzier-la-Caille

name: La Bauche
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 F3 La Bauche

name: Beaulieu
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 B3 Beaulieu

name: Le Bouchet-St-Nicolas
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 B4 Le Bouchet-St-Nicolas

name: Brides-les-Bains
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 H3 Brides-les-Bains

name: Bully
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D2 Bully

name: Chalencon
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D4 Chalencon

name: Chanac
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 A5 Chanac

name: Corlier
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 E1 Corlier

name: Desaignes
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D4 Desaignes

name: Fontans
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 A4 Fontans

name: Gabrias
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 A4 Gabrias

name: Laveyron
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D3 Laveyron

name: Lentilly
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D2 Lentilly

name: Mons Seleucus
description: Mons Seleucus was the site of the famous battle between Constantius II and the usurper Magnentius in AD 353. The Gallo-Roman vicus at La Bâtie-Montsaléon included a Gallo-Roman sanctuary.

name: La Motte-St-Martin
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 F4 La Motte-St-Martin

name: Neyrac-les-Bains
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 C4 Neyrac-les-Bains

name: Passy
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 H2 Passy

name: Semo Sancus, Aedes
description: A sanctuary on the Quirinal Hill in Rome sacred to the cult of Semo Sancus Dius Fidius, a cult of Sabine origin said to have been introduced to Rome by Titus Tatius. The dedication of the temple is generally attributed to Sp. Postumius on 5 June 466 BCE.

name: Temple of Athena Lindia
description: A Doric temple dating ca. 300 BC located on the acropolis of Lindos. The sanctuary stands above a grotto that likely housed the earlier phases of the cult. The amphiprostyle temple has an elongated floor plan and measures 7.75 x 21.65 m.

name: Honos et Virtus, Aedes
description: The Temple of Honour and Virtue (aedes Honoris et Virtutis) was a double temple founded by Q. Fabius Maximus Verrucosus in 234 BCE. The temple and mentions of it seem to have disappeared after the fourth century CE. It was located outside the Porta Capena (ad portam Capenam), likely on the north side of the Via Appia.

name: Mars, Aedes
description: A temple sacred to Mars located on the Via Appia. The shrine was located between the first and second milestones and was dedicated in the fourth century BC. According to Livy, Titus Quinctius dedicated the temple on 1 June 387 BC.

name: Mortuary temple of Mentuhotep II
description: The Mortuary temple of the Eleventh Dynasty pharaoh Mentuhotep II at Deir el-Bahari would inspire the later mortuary complexes of Hatshepsut and Thutmose III that are also found at the site.

name: Temple of Diana
description: The temple in the forum of Augusta Emerita is conventionally known by the misnomer "Temple of Diana", yet was actually dedicated to the Imperial cult.

name: Sanctuary of Cybèle
description: A Sanctuary of Cybèle located adjacent to the theater complex at Lugdunum and dating to the middle of the second century CE.

name: Sanctuary at Fondo Iozzino
description: An extra-urban sanctuary located outside of the Porta Nocera of Pompeii. Excavations at the sanctuary have revealed votive activity in multiple periods, including the pre-Roman phases of Pompeii. Inscribed bucchero pottery indicates connections with leading Etruscan families of central Italy.

name: Mithraeum at Riegel
description: A temple once sacred to Mithras located in Riegel am Kaiserstuhl (Baden-Wuerttemberg, Germany).

name: Tholos of Athena Pronaia
description: A tholos in the Doric order located in the temenos of Athena Pronaia at Delphi. The temple is dated ca. 380 - 370 BCE.

name: Prdejci
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 50 B2 Prdejci

name: Vojšanci
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 50 B1 Vojšanci

name: Zeus Olympios (temple)
description: The temple of Zeus Olympios on Mt. Olympus

name: Dagana
description: An ancient city of southern Sri Lanka. It was characterized by Ptolemy as "sacred to Selene", a description repeated in the Arabic geographic tradition. Its precise location is uncertain: BAtlDir echoes earlier suggestions (see RE) that tentatively identify it with Tangalla, but RE would prefer as "more likely" an identification with the temple complex and associated settlement at Tenavaram (modern Tongra), an identification also suggested by earlier scholars.

name: Kepia
description: The sanctuary of Heros Auloneites at Kepia.

name: Megaloi Theoi (sanctuary, Samothrace)
description: The "Sanctuary of the great gods" on the island of Samothrace, one of the chief Panhellenic sanctuaries of the ancient Greek world.

name: Oulippada?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 5 inset Oulippada?

name: *Templum Augusti
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 5 D4 *Templum Augusti

name: Delphinion
description: A sanctuary of Apollo Delphinios in Athens, which was closely associated with Theseus and served as a law court for cases of justifiable homicide. It is perhaps to be identified with the Classical Doric temple, whose remains are located south of the Olympieion.

name: Sanctuary of Artemis Brauronia on the Acropolis
description: The sanctuary of Artemis Brauronia on the Acropolis of Athens was a branch of the sanctuary of Artemis at Brauron in eastern Attica, perhaps established by Peisistratos or his sons in the second half of the sixth century BC, although the surviving structure probably dates from the 430s BC. It consisted of a Doric stoa with two wings surrounding a court, hosted a giant bronze statue of the Trojan horse by Strongylion, and was probably the site of the Arkteia ritual.

name: Terrace of Zeus
description: The so-called "Terrace of Zeus" or "Terrazza di Zeus" at Elea-Velia.

name: Sanctuary of Artemis Soteira in the Kerameikos
description: This sanctuary is located in the Kerameikos of Athens, south of the street of the tombs and was established in the early Hellenistic Age. It has often been identified as a sanctuary of Hekate, but more recent research suggests that it was dedicated to Artemis Soteira. It also contained a prophetic well associated with Paian.

name: Zingsheim
description: A Gallo-Roman temple of the Matronae Aufaniae.

name: Henchir Oum Iala
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F3 Henchir Oum Iala

name: Ksar Djaria
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F2 Ksar Djaria

name: Monopteros
description: A small round shrine built in the Athenian Agora in the mid-2nd century AD

name: Altar of Zeus Agoraios
description: An altar dedicated to Zeus Agoraios, originally erected on the Pnyx in the late fourth century BC, but relocated to the Athenian Agora in the Augustan period.

name: Sanctuary of Palaimon (Palaimonium)
description: The Palaimonion at Isthmia is a Roman sanctuary dedicated to an embodiment of the Isthmian games.

name: Khane Gauhar
description: Remains of a Buddhist shrine of uncertain date located near modern Darwishan in the Nawa-i-Barakzayi district of Afghanistan's Helmand province.

name: Temple of Hera, Mon Repos
description: An archaic temple dating ca. 610 B.C.

name: Five Rathas
description: The Five Rathas is the modern name of a group of five seventh-century Pallava rock-cut temples at Mahābalipuram.

name: Triopium
description: Sanctuary of Apollo Triopios, known as the Triopion, in the territory of Knidos, was the federal sanctuary of the Dorian hexapolis.

name: Sanctuary of Hercules Victor
description: The Sanctuary of Hercules Victor in Tivoli is a significant cult place of the second century B.C. The sanctuary is composed of a vaulted, terraced platform that support the temple, its porticoes, and a theater.

name: Monte d'Accoddi
description: A ramped, megalithic structure in Sardinia dated to around 2700-2000 BC.

name: Serapieion B at Delos
description: One of three shrines sacred to Serapis identified by archaeologists working at Delos in the nineteenth century.

name: Sanctuary of Artemis Aristoboule
description: A small temple of Artemis Aristoboule ("of best deliberation") erected by Themistokles

name: Temple of Venus
description: The Temple of Venus (VIII,1) is located adjacent to Pompeii's Porta Marina. The site was cleared in the early years of the colony to make way for the temple. Seriously damaged by the AD 62 earthquake, the sanctuary was still being rebuilt at the time of its ultimate destruction.

name: Sanctuary of Aphrodite Pandemos
description: The sanctuary of Aphrodite Pandemos ("of all the People") and Peitho ("Persuasion"), established by Theseus or Solon, was located on the south slope of the Acropolis of Athens, directly below the Nike Temple bastion. It was a centre of the Aphrodisia and Arrephoria festivals. The surviving remains belong to a structure built ca. 287/6 BCE.

name: Caesarea Mithraeum
description: The Mithraeum at Caesarea is only one of two excavated from the eastern half of the Roman empire.

name: Greek Temple at Taras
description: A Doric temple dating to the sixth century B.C., located in the modern piazza Castello in Taranto, Italy. It was initially attributed to Poseidon, but may have been dedicated to a different deity.

name: Temple to Ramesses II at Abu Simbel
description: The larger of the two temples at Abu Simbel, dedicated to the triad of Amun, Ra-Horakhty, and Ptah, as well as the deified Ramesses II. The temple was relocated between 1964 and 1968 to prevent its submersion under Lake Nasser after the construction of the Aswan High Dam.

name: Temple of Victory
description: A Greek temple of the Doric order (peripteral hexastyle), built in the fifth century BC, possibly to celebrate a victory against the Carthaginians. Its ruins are still visible in the archaeological site of Himera.

name: Temple of Artemis Azzanathkona
description: The temple of a syncretized incarnation of Artemis built at Dura-Europos in the Hellenistic period.

name: The Temple of Janus (Rome)
description: The now destroyed Temple of Janus near the Forum Romanum, Rome, was originally built in the reign of Numa, according to Livy.

name: Temple of Hathor
description: The Temple of Hathor at Deir el-Medina dates to the reign of the Ptolemies. It was later repurposed as a Christian installation known as Deir el-Medina or "Monastery of the City", from which the site's modern toponym is derived.

name: Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut
description: The Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut at Deir el-Bahari was built for the Eighteenth dynasty Pharaoh Hatshepsut.

name: Temple of Artemis, Jerash
description: The Temple of Artemis in Jerash.

name: Kybele Sanctuary at Kapikaya
description: The Kybele Sanctuary at Kapikaya is a rock-cut sanctuary sacred to Kybele, approximately 6 km from Pergamon, and is included in the UNESCO World Heritage site "Pergamon and its Multi-Layered Cultural Landscape".

name: Punic-Roman Sanctuary, Kamart
description: A Punic-Roman sanctuary, possibly of Hygieia/Salus or Aesculapius/Eshmun was identified to the northwest of Carthage in the direction of Cape Kamart (Tunisia), mostly on the basis of an assemblage of extremely fragmentary polychromatic terracotta statues, numbering at least 30.

name: Mercurius, Aedes
description: A temple dedicated to Mercury and Maia that was located on the slopes of the Aventine Hill overlooking the Circus Maximus.

name: Tholos at the Sanctuary of Asklepios, Epidauros
description: The Tholos, a circular structure in the Sanctuary of Asklepios at Epidaurus, was constructed between 365 and 360 BCE. Polykleitos the Younger designed it, and it features Doric architecture and a constructed labyrinth beneath its floor.

props.ancientName: Tholos at the Sanctuary of Asklepios, Epidaurus

name: Temple of the Storm God at Aleppo
description: Bronze Age temple dedicated to the weather god Hadad in the citadel of Halab (modern Aleppo).

name: Great Temple of Hera
description: The Great Temple of Hera, or Polycrates Temple, is the third temple constructed at the Heraion of Samos between 540 and 530 BCE.

name: Temple of Mars Ultor
description: The Temple of Mars Ultor was dedicated in the Forum of Augustus in 2 B.C., fulfilling a vow that Octavian had made to the god in 42 B.C.

name: Mdoukha
description: Roman sanctuary with two small temples.

name: Sanctuary of Demeter Chamyne
description: A sanctuary of the fifth century B.C. located to the east of the stadium at Olympia.

name: Sanctuary of Artemis at Avlonas
description: A sanctuary possibly sacred to Artemis on the island of Lemnos.

name: Philippeion
description: The Philippeion is a tholos at Olympia constructed to celebrate Philip II's victory at Chaeonea in 338 BCE and completed by Alexander the Great.

name: Temple of Hathor Miskar
description: A Punic temple founded in the first century B.C.

name: templum divi Claudii
description: A temple dedicated to the emperor Claudius who was declared divus (deified) after his death in 54 CE. The platform temple occupied a large area of the Caelian Hill in Rome. The construction project was ultimately completed by the Flavians.

name: Metroon at Olympia
description: Temple at Olympia constructed in the late 4th or early 3rd century BCE

name: Ellès
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 33 D1 Ellès

name: Soumat el Kheneg
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 33 B2 Soumat el Kheneg

name: Tossal del Moro
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 F4 Tossal del Moro

name: Colonnes de Riez
description: The remains of a Roman temple of the first century CE.

name: Sanctuary of Asklepios, Balagrae
description: The Sanctuary of Asklepios at Balagrae in Cyrenaica is dated to the Hadrianic period on the basis of epigraphic evidence.

name: Tongeren Gallo-Roman temple complex
description: The Gallo-Roman temple complex of Tongeren is located in the northwestern part of the city of Tongeren. The site pre-dates the Roman city wall.

name: Ash Altar of Zeus Lykaios
description: The ash altar of Zeus Lykaios on the southern peak of Mt. Lykaion.

name: Temple of Amenhotep II
description: Amenhotep II built a temple at Giza that he dedicated to the god Horemakhet, a sun-god identified with the Great Sphinx. It lies northeast of the Sphinx itself.

name: Sail-sur-Couzan
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 B2 Sail-sur-Couzan

name: Ste-Abeille
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 C4 Ste-Abeille

name: St-Alban-les-Eaux

It is archaeologically represented by a two-part architectural complex comprising (1) north of the modern town the monumental "Pont des Arches", which supported a temple over a watercourse that supplied a large spa, and (2) at the nearby Lac-d'Antre, which supplied the majority of the water for the spa complex and also hosted additional temples along its shoreline. name: Temple of Isis
description: The Temple of Isis in Pompeii (VIII, 7, 28) is a sanctuary sacred to Isis that dates from the second century BC.

name: Duino Mithraeum
description: A Mithraeum near Duino, in the province of Trieste, north-eastern Italy, that is located within a natural cave.

name: Temple of Athena Nike
description: A temple dedicated to Athena Nike, on a bastion at the southwest end of the Acropolis of Athens. A sanctuary existed from the early 6th century BC, a small shrine was built in the mid-5th century, and the temple was built between 449 and 420 BCE. It was dismantled by the Ottomans in 1686 and reassembled between 1835 and 1844.

name: Tarxien (Malta)
description: A megalithic temple complex that was named as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1980. This temple dates to the later Neolithic period on Malta and remained in use for several millennia. The first usage of the site occurs ca. 4100 BC and the temple complex develops across the fourth millennium.

name: Sanctuary of Apollo at Halieis
description: The Sanctuary of Apollo at Halieis is a temple complex dedicated primarily to Apollo, constructed outside of the city of Halieis and dating between the seventh and fourth centuries BCE.

name: Temple of Olympian Zeus, Syracuse
description: The Temple of Olympian Zeus is located approximately 3 km southwest of Syracuse on the Cyane river.

name: Tempio di Matzanni
description: The remains of a Punic temple located in the Matzanni archaeological area.

name: Anemospilia
description: A Minoan temple (?) located on northern end of Mount Iuktas.

name: Doric temple at Kaulonia
description: The Doric Temple at Caulonia has been dated ca. 420 B.C.

name: Portonaccio sanctuary at Veii
description: The extraurban sanctuary of Minerva at Portonaccio is an important Etruscan cult center. The temple dates ca. 510 B.C. and was adorned with acroterial sculptures, notably the famous "Apollo of Veii".

name: Ezida (Kalhu)
description: When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the god Nabû. Unlike most of the religious buildings constructed at Kalhu, the Ezida ("True House") was built in the southeast corner of the Citadel.

name: Temple of Hermes at Çatıören
description: Temple of Hermes at Çatıören

name: Valley Temple of Khafre
description: The Valley Temple associated with the pyramid of Khafre. The Valley Temple is linked to the pyramid and its mortuary temple via a causeway.

name: Egalmah
description: The temple of the goddess Gula (as Ninisinna) at Isin. The Sumerian ceremonial name Egalmah means "Exalted Palace."

name: Parthenon
description: The temple of Athena Parthenos located on the Acropolis of Athens, which was constructed at the height of Athenian power between 447 and 432 BCE. It is the largest Doric temple in mainland Greece.

name: Temple of Aphrodite on the Acrocorinth
description: A temple to Aphrodite on the Acrocorinth likely constructed in the 5th century BCE. According to Plutarch, the women of Corinth prayed to the statue of the armed Aphrodite to instil the 'eros' of battle in their men during the Greco-Persian war. The armed Aphrodite was a significant icon and motif of the Acrocorinthian temple, that continued after the destruction of Corinth by the Romans in 146 BCE. The temple is also famous for supposed sacred prostitution, as described by Strabo, but these claims are unverified.

name: Temple d'Appolon
description: The ruins of a Roman-period temple dedicated to Apollo located at the Oppidum de Gaujac (Gard, France).

name: Almendres Cromlech
description: A megalithic complex located near Guadalupe, Portgual, dating to the Early Neolithic period.

name: Temple of Sardus Pater

name: Ajanta Caves
description: The Ajanta Caves are among the earliest Buddhist cave monuments and date from the second and first centuries B.C. During the Gupta period (fifth and sixth centuries A.D.), additional decorated caves were added to the group.

name: Temple of Jupiter
description: A temple dedicated to the Capitoline triad is located on the forum of Saepinum.

name: Chale Ghar 1
description: Bronze Age copper mine reused as a sanctuary or ritual site from the 8th century BCE-8th century CE, located near the modern village of Veshnaveh, Iran.

name: Sanctuary of Kybele

name: Etemenanki
description: The ziggurat of the god Marduk at Babylon. The Sumerian ceremonial name Etemenanki means "House, Foundation Platform of Heaven and Underworld."

name: Emašdari
description: The temple of the goddess Ištar of Agade at Babylon. The Sumerian ceremonial name Emašdari means "House of Animal Offerings." The Neo-Babylonian king Nabonidus rebuilt/renovated it.

name: Ekunankuga
description: The ziggurat of the god Šamaš at Sippar. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ekunankuga means "House, Pure Stairway of Heaven."

name: Eduranna
description: The ziggurat of the god Šamaš at Larsa. The Sumerian ceremonial name Eduranna means "House, Bond of Heaven."

name: Temple of Artemis (Thasos)
description: Temple of Artemis on Thasos Island with remains from the Archaic period to the 1st century CE.

name: Temple of Athena (Miletus)
description: The Temple of Athena in Miletos dates originally to the sixth century BCE, although the visible remains on site today belong to the Classical rebuilding of MIletos (ca. 479-450 BCE). The temple had a peripteral plan and was executed in the Ionic order. Unusually, its axis is oriented north-south.

name: Temple of Aššur at Assur
description: From the second half of the third millennium BC until 614 BC, there was a temple in Assur dedicated to the city’s tutelary deity Aššur. The temple was excavated by W. Andrae in 1903–05 and 1909–12. The earliest and latest textual records for construction work on Aššur’s temple date respectively to the time of Šalim-aḫum, an early old Assyrian king, and the reign of Ashurbanipal (668–631 BC), Assyria’s last great king. According to the Middle Assyrian king Shalmaneser I (1263–1234 BC) and the Neo-Assyrian ruler Esarhaddon (680–669 BC), Ušpia, a tribal ancestor of the Old Assyrian king Šamšī-Adad I (ca. 1808–1776 BC), was the building’s founder. In 614 BC, when the city was captured by the Medes, the temple was destroyed.

name: Artemis Laphria Sanctuary (Kalydon)
description: This sanctuary is believed to have been active since at least the Geometric period, possibly on the site of a Mycenaean settlement. Ceramic evidence indicates continuous activity at least through the Roman period.

name: Güglingen Mithraeum
description: A sanctuary dedicated to Mithras located near modern Heilbronn, Germany, in use during the second-third centuries CE.

name: Temple of Anu and Adad at Assur
description: A distinctive feature of the city of Assur is the double-temple of the gods Anu and Adad. This building, together with its twin ziggurats, was constructed no later than the Middle Assyrian period; it underwent a major rebuilding in the reign of Tiglath-pileser I (1114–1076 BC). The existence of an (unnamed) Adad temple at Assur, however, is known from inscriptions of the Old Assyrian rulers Erišum I and Ikūnum. The various parts of this axisymmetric, double-temple was given Sumerian names: Anu’s temple and ziggurat were called Ešaan and Emelamanna; and Adad’s temple and ziggurat went by the names Ešuddegeštuku and Egišlamšaršar.

name: Brihadishwara Temple
description: 11th century Hndu temple Tanjuvar dedicated to Shiva with smaller shrines around it.

name: Emašmaš
description: The most important temple in the citadel of Nineveh was dedicated to the city's tutelary deity, the goddess Ištar. The temple was located in the center of the citadel and went by the Sumerian ceremonial name Emašmaš (meaning uncertain).

name: Mithraeum of the Seven Spheres
description: The so-called Mithraeum of the Seven Spheres is located in ancient Ostia near to the theater-cum-quadriporticus complex. The so-called Quattro tempietti screen it from the Decumanus Maximus.

name: Sanctuary of the Bona Dea
description: The Sanctuary of the Bona Dea at Ostia dates to the republican period (second century BCE?) and was renovated in the second century CE.

name: Quattro tempietti
description: The sanctuary along the Decumanus Maximus of Ostia known as the "Quattro tempietti" dates from the Republican period. Based on multiple phases of restoration, they continued in use until at least the second century CE. Epigraphic evidence leads some scholars to conclude that the four temples were sacred to Venus, Fortuna, Ceres, and Spes and had been dedicated by one Publius Lucilius Gamala.

name: Kynosarges
description: A gymnasium and sanctuary of Herakles at Athens, located southeast of the Acropolis

name: Temple of Hercules

name: Qalaat Faqra
description: A Roman-era sanctuary complex in Lebanon with multiple altars and two temples. On the basis of epigraphic evidence, one of these temples seems to have been dedicated to Atargatis and the other possibly to Zeus Beelgalassos. There is some evidence for worship also of Zeus Heliopolis and for the Roman imperial cult. A Byzantine basilica was later built on the site.

name: Piana del Lago Sanctuary
description: An Etruscan sanctuary that was founded at the end of the 5th century BCE and saw activity as late as the reign of Nero. Evidence has also been found for activity in the area during the Neolithic and the Bronze Age. The site is located on the southern shore of the Lac de Bolsena in the modern Italian municipality of Montefiascone.

name: Friars Wash Roman Temple
description: The Friars Wash Roman Temple was noted first in 1965. Aerial photography and subsequent archaeological investigation identified a system of ditches and two rectangular structures that were considered to be Romano-British temples.

name: Temple of Athena at Assos
description: A Late Archaic peripteral temple in the Doric order with a 6 x 13 columnar arrangement.

name: Khajuraho Group of Monuments
description: The Khajuraho Group of Monuments is designated as a UNESCO World Heritage site located in the modern Indian state of Madhya Pradesh. These temples were built during the Chandella dynasty (ca. 950-1050 CE). The remaining temples belong to two different religions – Hinduism and Jainism

name: Temple of Apollo at Syracuse

name: Temple of Peltuinum
description: The Temple of Peltuinum is located on the forum square; its final iteration was that of a Corinthian hexastyle prostyle sanctuary of the Roman phase of the city, when the shrine may have been dedicated to Apollo. The sanctuary also may have had a pre-Roman phase connected with a sacred spring.

name: Mandra (Despotiko)
description: A substantial multiperiod sanctuary and associated complex on the Aegean island of Despotiko. Structures and finds date from the early Iron Age through Roman times, with some evidence for use or reuse into the Byzantine period.

name: Koi Krylgan Kala
description: Koi Krylgan Kala is an archaeological site in Uzbekistan. At the site are the remains of a temple complex associated with the Chorasmian Dynasty, which was destroyed approximately 200 BC.

name: Alcántara
description: The Roman temple of Alcántara is a well-preserved structure located at one side of the Alcántara Bridge in Extremadura (Spain). The building, dating to A.D. 103, is the work of Caius Iulius Lacer. The lintel inscription indicates the structure was dedicated to Trajan.
