name: Delphinion (Miletos)
description: The Hellenistic- and Roman-period sanctuary of Apollo Delphinios is located northeast of the North Market at Miletos.

name: Nymphaeum of Egeria
description: A nymphaeum connected with Egeria, the divine advisor of Numa Pompilius. This iteration of Egeria's spring dates to the time of Herodes Atticus in the second century A.D.

name: Temple of Aphrodite Euploia
description: The temple of Aphrodite Euploia is a tholos constructed in the fourth century BC in Knidos, a coastal city on the Datça Peninsula. It was built to house Praxiteles' famous nude statue of Aphrodite.

name: Temple of Liber Pater at Sabratha
description: A Temple of Liber Pater at Sabratha that dates to the Iulio-Claudian period.

name: Temple of Hera II (Neptune)
description: A fifth century B.C. Doric temple, the so-called Temple of Hera II is also sometimes referred to as the Temple of Neptune.

name: Ehilikalamma
description: The temple of the goddess Ašratu, Ehilikalamma (“House of the Luxuriance of the Land”), was located in the Ka-dingirra district (modern Sahn) of Babylon. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, this was one of four temples in that part of the city, which was located in the northern part of the eastern half of Babylon.

name: Aphrodision at Akrai
description: Temple dedicated to Aphrodite constructed in the 6th century BCE at the Greek colony of Akrai.

name: Sanctuary of Apollo Pythios
description: A Doric-order temple with a hexastyle in antis plan measuring 31.15x16.05 m. According to Mazarakis-Ainian, the cella is divided into two parts by means of an axial colonnade. The altar stood at the east front of the temple.

name: Sanctuary at Agios Konstantinos (Methana)
description: A Late Helladic sanctuary was excavated by Helene Konstolakis-Jiannopoulou in 1990 near the chapel of Agios Konstantinos and Elenis. Activity at this site dates between 1500 and 1300 B.C.

name: Iuktas
description: A Minoan peak sanctuary on Juktas that served the Minoan palace of Knossos. The site was first excavated in 1909 by Sir Arthur Evans. The site was in use from Early to Late Bronze Age.

name: Sanctuary of Apollo
description: The Sanctuary of Apollo near Amyklai is a stoa-like building that enclosed a colossal statue of Apollo on three sides and was decorated with reliefs. The tomb of Hyacinthus is used as a statue base, combining the two local deities together.

name: Temple of Apollo Patroos
description: The Temple of Apollo Patroos on the west side of the Agora of Athens was built at the end of the fourth century BC. It contained a famous statue of Apollo by Euphranor. The structure wraps around the tiny fourth-century BC shrine of Zeus Phratrios and Athena Phratrios. The existence of an earlier temple, destroyed during the Persian Sack of 480 BC, is disputed.

name: Eurysakeion
description: A sanctuary dedicated to Eurysakes, son of Ajax on the Kolonos Agoraios in Athens, which served as the central sanctuary of the tribe Aiantis. It has not been located archaeologically, but the findspots of related inscriptions suggest it was somewhere southwest of the Hephaisteion.

name: Hathor Temple, Timna Valley
description: A shrine sacred to Hathor connected with the Egyptian mining settlement that was extracting copper in the Timna Valley.

name: Pagania Sanctuary (Aigies, Laconia)
description: A rural sanctuary, probably of Artemis, located about a kilometer north-northwest of modern Aigies in Laconia. It is surely to be associated with ancient Aigiai, which was located less than two kilometers to the southwest.

name: Sanctuary of Zeus Aphesios (Megarid)
description: Pausanias mentions the Sanctuary of Zeus Aphesios as being located atop a mountain. The site was excavated ca. 1890 and has views over the Saronic Gulf.

props.ancientName: Dios Aphesiou naos, Diós Aphesíou naós

name: Pythion
description: The sanctuary of Apollo Pythios in Athens, located south of the Temple of Olympian Zeus, probably near Ioseph ton Rogon street, where many inscriptions related to the cult have been found.

name: Sphinx Temple
description: The Old Kingdom temple associated with the Great Sphinx of Giza and assigned to the reign of Khafre.

name: Hiera Oikia at Eleusis
description: The so-called "Hiera Oikia" is a multi-room structure dating to the 7th century BC in the sanctuary of Demeter and Kore at Eleusis.

name: Temple of Athena Pronaia
description: The Temple of Athena Pronaia, located at Delphi, is a peripteral Doric building (6 x 12 columns). It was built ca. 500 BC and is the largest building in the sanctuary of Athena Pronaia.

name: Mithraeum at Mundelsheim
description: A second-century Mithraeum is located in the area of the municipality of Mundelsheim in the Ludwigsburg district of Baden-Württemberg, Germany.

name: Aedes Hercules Musarum
description: Marcus Fulvius Nobilior likely built this temple in Rome's Circus Flaminius ca. 187 B.C. following his campaigns in Ambracia. In 29 B.C., the temple was restored by L. Marcius Philippus and included in the new porticus bearing his name.

name: Temple of Romulus (Rome)
description: A temple along the Sacra Via dedicated to Valerius Romulus, son of Maxentius.

name: Abini nuragic sanctuary
description: A nuragic sanctuary.

name: Gallo-Roman sites at Érome, France
description: A pair of archaeological sites in Gallia Narbonensis. The site of Les Orpaillots shows traces of a sanctuary and a group of tombs while Notre-Dame-de-la-Mure showed traces of a late third century Gallo-Roman villa.

name: Gallo-Roman remains at La Tour, Sablé-sur-Sarthe
description: The remains of a Gallo-Roman sanctuary.

name: Comblessac
description: A heptagonal Gallo-Roman temple.

name: Sanctuaries of Apollo Deiradiotes and Athena Oxyderkes
description: At Argos, Pausanias mentions both the sanctuary of Apollo Deiradiotes (Apollo of the ridge) and the sanctuary of Athena Oxyderkes (Athena the clear-sighted).

name: Castellu di Cucuruzzu
description: Castellu di Cucuruzzu is a Torrean archaeological site in Corsica.

name: Santu Misirinu
description: An octagonal structure covered by a cupola, dating between the second and sixth centuries A.D.

name: Ishtar Temple at Mari
description: Temple dedicated to the goddess Ishtar located in the western section of the city of Mari.

name: Timpone della Motta
description: The Timpone della Motta is an archaeological site located near Francavilla Marittima. The site was inhabited from the Middle Bronze Age onward. During the Iron Age, an Oenotrian settlement flourished here, one that developed into an important sanctuary. The site passed into Greek control in the later seventh century B.C. and was eventually abandoned following the Bruttian conquest of the region during the fourth century B.C.

name: Circus Maximus Mithraeum
description: A mithraeum adjacent to the Circus Maximus and facing the carceres of the same was located at the margin of the Forum Boarium and now situated beneath a building in the via dell'Ara Massima. The site was excavated in 1931.

name: Temple of Quirinus
description: The Temple of Quirinus was located near the Capitolium Vetus. The temple was built ex manubiis by Lucius Papirius Cursor in 293 B.C., fulfilling the vow made by his father in 325 B.C. Tradition holds that the republican-period temple was sited on an even earlier altar dedicated by the Sabines. The topography and location of the temple are both matters of significant debate.

name: Mortuary Temple of Ramesses III at Medinet Habu
description: The New Kingdom mortuary temple of Ramesses III at Medinet Habu, on the West bank of the Nile near Luxor.

name: Temple de Diane
description: The so-called "Temple de Diane" is a first century Roman monument on the Gard river dedicated to Augustus.

name: Temple of Concordia, Agrigento
description: The Temple of Concord, located in the so-called "Valle dei Templi" of Agrigento, dates ca. 440-430 B.C.

name: Temple of Hercules at Amman/Philadelpheia
description: The Temple of Hercules is located within the Amman Citadel in Amman, Jordan. The temple was built during the administration of Geminius Marcianus, ca. 162-166.

name: Sacellum of the Augustales
description: The so-called "Sacellum of the Augustales" at Misenum is an architectural complex located near the theater that was first built during the Iulio-Claudian period and dedicated to the Imperial cult. The complex was renovated during the second century, at which time one Cassia Victoria re-dedicated the shrine in honor of her husband, L. Laecanius Primitivus, who was a priest of the Augustales during the reign of Marcus Aurelius.

name: Ġgantija
description: Megalithic temple on the island of Gozo; named a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1980

name: Garden of Cybele
description: An archaeological area in Vienne that includes remains of municipal and domestic architecture.

name: Barzan (Gallo-Roman Site)
description: Site of a Gallo-Roman sanctuary and subsequent city of the Roman imperial period, the "Site gallo-romain de Barzan" is spread across the modern French communes of Barzan, Talmont-sur-Gironde and Arces-sur-Gironde in the department of Charente-Maritime. BAtlas equated it with ancient Tannum/Tamnum, but alternatives continue to be offered.

name: Unnamed sanctuary at Garenne
description: In modern Moulin des Gaumins in commune Thaumiers, France (BAtlas 14 H2 Thaumiers)

name: Tour de Gresset
description: The remains of a Gallo-Roman fanum.

name: Vietheil
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 G3 Vietheil

name: Temple of Khnum at Esna
description: A temple dedicated to the god Khnum constructed during the Ptolemaic and Roman period in the city of Esna (Latopolis).

name: Temple of Artemis Agrotera
description: A temple of Artemis on the Ilissos that marks the spot where the goddess first hunted after arriving from Delos. The archaeological remains of the site are found on the modern Ardettou Street in Athens.

props.ancientName: Naos Agroteras Artemidos, Naós Agrotéras Artémidos

name: Palladion
description: A sanctuary in Attica, containing the Palladion, an image of Athena captured during the Trojan War by the Argives, who were killed by Damophon and the Athenians on their way home. The sanctuary was the location of a lawcourt for cases of involuntary homicide, attempted homicide, and homicide of a foreigner, resident foreigner or enslaved person. It was probably located in south-eastern Athens or perhaps in Phaleron.

name: Older Parthenon
description: A large temple on the Acropolis of Athens, begun in the 480s BC and destroyed during the Persian Sack of 480 BC, while still under construction. Parts of the structure were incorporated into the north wall of the Acropolis and other structures in the mid-fifth century BC and the Parthenon was built on the same site, using some of the older structure's foundations, beginning in 447 BC.

name: Kekropeion
description: The tomb and shrine of Kekrops, first king of Athens, located on the Acropolis. In the sixth century BC it was marked by a giant Ionic column, which was destroyed in the Persian Sack of 480 BC. The site was partially covered by the southwestern corner of the Erechtheion in the second half of the fifth century BC.

name: Temple of Apollo
description: The Temple of Apollo (VII,7,32) in Pompeii is one of the city's oldest cults, dating to the sixth century BC. The sanctuary assumed its present appearance in the second century BC.

name: Apollo, Aedes
description: The likely site of the original sanctuary to Apollo at Rome. The visible remains belong to the Augustan phase.

name: Ta Prohm
description: Khmer temple at Angkor constructed in 1186 CE under Jayavarman VII.

name: Sanctuary of Themis
description: A sanctuary of Themis (order, righteousness), located on the south slope of the Acropolis of Athens.

name: Roman Temple at Maarab
description: Site of a Roman temple overlooking Jounieh Bay

name: Temple of Ishtar at Assur
description: From the late Early Dynastic period (ED III, ca. 2500/2400 BC) until 614 BC, there was a temple in the city of Assur dedicated to Ištar, a goddess who was revered in that important northern-Mesopotamian city in a few different forms. Several building phases (designated “Temples H–A,” from earliest to latest) were excavated by W. Andrae between 1905 and 1914. Periodically the location and orientation of the Ištar temple changed, especially during the reigns of the Middle Assyrian kings Tukultī-Ninurta I (1243–1207 BC) and Aššur-rēša-iši I (1132–1115 BC). The earliest and latest textual records for the existence of the temple date respectively to the time of Ititi, a local ruler of Assur during the Old Akkadian Period, and the reign of Sîn-šarra-iškun (626–612 BC), the penultimate king of Assyria.

name: Castiglione del Lago
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 C2 Castiglione del Lago

name: Le Ciliegeta
description: The site of Le Ciliegeta, near the source of the river Arno, served as a rich stips votiva. The site was discovered in 1836. The finds included over 600 bronzes, more than 1,000 pieces of aes rude, 2,000 weapons, and other artifacts.

name: La Cuma, Monte Rinaldo
description: The principal archaeological remains found in the "La Cuma, Monte Rinaldo" site are those of a late Republican sanctuary dating to the third through first centuries B.C.

name: Fons Clitumni
description: Originally the site of a shrine dedicated to the river god Clitumnus and later a paleochristian shrine. The temple was listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2011.

name: *Hercules Curinus
description: A sanctuary in the territory of the Paeligni dedicated to Hercules Curinus situated on an artificial terrace on the slopes of Mount Morrone. The terrace sanctuary looks over Sulmo in the valley below.

name: *Incerulae
description: *Incerulae, an ancient sanctuary site in the territory of the Vestini.

name: Nemora Vacunae
description: Shrine dedicated to a rural divinity of the Sabina.

name: Pescara
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 F4 Pescara

name: Raiano
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 F4 Raiano

name: S. Anatolia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 E4 S. Anatolia

name: Fanum Voltumnae
description: A shrine of Voltumna that served as the chief religious center of Etruria. The Etruscan League (duodecim populi Etruriae) was said to meet annually at the shrine. Archaeological excavations of the site of Campo della Fiera have been posited as the definitive location of the Fanum Voltumnae.

name: Aefula
description: Aefula was an ancient settlement of Latium that had disappeared by the time of Pliny the Elder, although the cult of Bona Dea there continued to flourish.

name: Caere
description: Caere (also known as Cisra or Cerveteri) was a major Etruscan polis located ca. 50 km north-northwest of Rome in southern Etruria.

name: Ceres et Faustina (temple)
description: The Roman temple dedicated to Ceres and Faustina received patronage from Herodes Atticus. Its remains were eventually repurposed in the construction of the church of Sant'Urbano.

name: Compitum
description: A multi-period sanctuary near Anagnia.

name: Crepaddosso
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 D3 Crepaddosso

name: Dea Dia (temple)
description: The Arval Brethren cultivated the cult of the Dea Dia. The remains of this Augustan era temple, located at La Tavernaccia restaurant in the Magliana Vecchia neighborhood of Rome, are subterranean and not open to the public.

name: Temple of Diana at Nemi
description: A site sacred to Diana near Lake Nemi, comprised of a Tuscan-style sanctuary and a sacred grove.

name: Fortuna Muliebris, T.
description: A sanctuary dedicated to Fortuna Muliebris along the Via Latina. Tradition holds that it was dedicated in 487 B.C.

name: Iuppiter Latiaris, T.
description: An important Latial sanctuary of Iuppiter located on Mons Albanus (modern Monte Cavo).

name: Monte Tosto
description: An Etruscan and Roman site lying 4 km to the west of Caere along the monumental road leading to Pyrgi. Until the early 1960s, the site was known for the monumental tumulus that Mengarelli had excavated in the 1920s. Subsequent excavation revealed other evidence, including an elite compound that served as a sanctuary.

name: Praeneste
description: An ancient city of central Italy, the eighth and seventh century B.C. origins of which demonstrate high-level trade contact with the Eastern Mediterranean world. The city withdrew from the Latin League (499 B.C.) and opposed Rome in the Latin war; Cincinnatus eventually subdued Praeneste. Sulla founded a new colony there in the first century B.C.

name: Robigo?, T.
description: The sanctuary of the god Robigus, located at the sixth mile of the Via Cassia. The Romans celebrated the festival of Robigalia on 25 April.

name: Tredici Altari
description: A Latin federal sanctuary at Lavinium, consisting of a series of altars that range in date from the sixth to the second century BC and a sacred site (or heroon) connected with the hero Aeneas.

name: Temple of Zeus Megistos, Canatha
description: A tetrastyle temple in the Corinthian order sacred to Zeus Megistos was built at Canatha by the first century CE.

name: Altar of Hieron II
description: A monumental altar built around the third century B.C. that was probably dedicated to Zeus Eleutherios. Remains of the altar are located in the archaeological area of Neapolis in Syracuse, Sicily.

props.ancientName: Ara di Ierone, Ara di Ierone II

name: Preah Khan
description: Temple at Angkor dedicated in 1191 CE under Jayavarman VII. The temple entered a period of decline beginning in the 15th century.

name: Ganesh Ratha
description: Modern name of a seventh-century Pallava rock-cut temple at Mahābalipuram.

name: Temple of Zeus Lepsinos at Euromus
description: The Temple of Zeus Lepsinos at Euromus is located southeast of the ancient settlement. The well-preserved Corinthian temple dates to the reign of Hadrian.

name: Trajan's Kiosk
description: The ancient monument referred to as "Trajan's Kiosk" or the "Pharaoh's Bed" is an incomplete hypaethral temple that dates to the second century CE. The monument is currently located on Agilkia Island although it was originally built on the island of Philae. It was relocated in the 1960s as part of the International Campaign to Save the Monuments of Nubia.

name: Nubian Lion Temple at Naqa
description: The Temple of Apedemak, also known as the Lion Temple, is located to the west of the temple of Amun.

name: So-called Temple 1 at Hattusa
description: Temple 1, also referred to as the "Great Temple", is located in the lower city of Hattusa. It dates to the thirteenth century B.C.

name: Temple of the Severan family
description: Near a bath complex built under Commodus, a Severan complex that included a plaza, porticoes, and a temple dedicated to the Severan family was added.

name: Lyceum
description: A sanctuary of Apollo Lyceios in Athens, which served as the seat of Aristotle's Peripatetic school of philosophy founded in 335/4 BCE. The Lyceum was destroyed by Sulla in 86 BCE.

name: Roman temple of Vic
description: The Roman temple of Vic dates to the second century A.D.

name: Panóias
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 D3 Panóias

name: S. Eulalia de Bóveda
description: Located some 16 km from Lugo, S. Eulalia de Bóveda is the location of a vaulted, three-aisled, underground building, likely dating to the middle Imperial period and functioning as a nymphaeum.

name: Belbèze
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 G2 Belbèze

name: Boades
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 G4 Boades

name: Camp-Ferrus
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 G1 Camp-Ferrus

name: Can Modolell
description: An ancient rural sanctuary of Hispania (Cabrera de Mar, Barcelona).

name: Ermita de S. Valero
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 F4 Ermita de S. Valero

name: Lloret
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 H4 Lloret

name: Malla
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 H4 Malla

name: Mézin
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 F1 Mézin

name: Nistos
description: A sanctuary on Mont Sacon at an elevation of 1541 meters above sea level.

name: Sacoué
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 F2 Sacoué

name: St-Béat
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 F3 St-Béat (1)

name: S. Cristina d’Aro
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 I4 S. Cristina d’Aro

name: S. Jorge
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 D3 S. Jorge

name: Sofuentes
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 D3 Sofuentes

name: Temple of Divus Iulius
description: The temple on the Sacra Via dedicated to the deified Iulius Caesar, authorized in 42 B.C. and built by Augustus, with its dedication occurring on 18th August, 29 B.C.

name: Temple of Artemis Orthosia
description: The Temple of Artemis Orthosia at Epidauros was founded in the Archaic period and continued in use in the Roman period. Pausanias mentions it as being on the city's temples.

name: Temple of Augustus and Rome (Ankara)
description: The Temple of Augustus and Roma at Ankara likely dates ca. 20 BCE. It is also known as the "Monumentum Ancyranum", highlighting the structure's role in preserving the most complete copy of the inscription known as the "Res Gestae Divi Augusti", which was promulgated in both ancient Greek and Latin following the death of emperor Augustus in 14 CE.

name: Surb Tadeos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 G1 Surb Tadeos

name: Guzana (Tell Halaf)
description: An Aramaean principality and Neo-Assyrian provincial capital founded on the site of an extinct Neolithic settlement. Settled through Hellenistic times, it was abandoned at the end of the first century BCE.

name: Zarnuqa
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 E3 Zarnuqa

name: Takht-i Bilqis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 90 C4 Takht-i Bilqis

name: Burj al-Qa'im
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 91 E3 Burj al-Qa'im

name: Rijelat Umm Kubar
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 91 C4 Rijelat Umm Kubar

name: Changiyeh
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 B3 Changiyeh

name: Gilan Gharbi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 A2 Gilan Gharbi

name: Khurramdasht
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 G3 Khurramdasht

name: Kushk Khainfar
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 B3 Kushk Khainfar

name: Lang-i Rud
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 G2 Lang-i Rud

name: Maimeh
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 B3 Maimeh

name: Naisar
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 G3 Naisar

name: Natanz
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 G3 Natanz

name: Navish
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 F2 Navish

name: Pusht-i Arisht
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 B3 Pusht-i Arisht

name: Seh Pa Aivan
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 B3 Seh Pa Aivan

name: War Pil
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 B3 War Pil

name: Kom el-Nana
description: Area south of the main city of Amarna that was the site of a temple during the Amarna period. The structure was overbuilt by a Christian monastery in late antiquity.

name: Kordin III
description: Megalithic temple in Malta constructed during the Mġarr phase (ca. 3700 BCE) and abandoned ca. 2500 BCE.

name: Skyphoi sanctuary
description: A sanctuary north of the Acropolis of Athens, consisting of a platform of the Archaic period, and 200 miniature skyphoi from the 3rd century BC. It is disputed whether it was an independent sanctuary or part of the sanctuary of Eros and Aphrodite.

name: [Augusta] Ambianorum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 B2 [Augusta] Ambianorum

name: Bad Bertrich
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 H2 Bad Bertrich

name: Bailleul-sur-Thérain
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 C3 Bailleul-sur-Thérain

name: Bracquemont
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 B3 Bracquemont

name: Gallo-Roman Sanctuary at Bû
description: Remains of a rectangular Romano-Celtic temple were excavated north of the French town of Bû (in the department of Eure-et-Loir), beginning in the 1960s. Other buildings of uncertain nature and a theater were also identified in the vicinity. Coins and pottery discovered at the site indicate there was activity there during the Roman imperial period.

name: Champlieu
description: A sanctuary located at the boundary of the civitas of the Suessiones and that of the Silvanectes.

name: Deneuvre
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G4 Deneuvre

name: Deuilly
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 D4 Deuilly

name: Domburg
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 D1 Domburg

name: Epiais-Rhus
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 C3 Epiais-Rhus

name: Epoissum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 F3 Epoissum

name: Estrées-St-Denis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 C3 Estrées-St-Denis

name: Fluy
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 C3 Fluy

name: Gamaches-en-Vexin
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 B3 Gamaches-en-Vexin

name: *Ganuenta?
description: Colijnsplaat, The Netherlands, is the site of an ancient sanctuary dedicated to Nehalennia.

name: Les Vaux de la Celle
description: An ancient Gallo-Roman sanctuary near modern Genainville (France), with excavated remains of a temple, nymphaeum, and theater: BAtlas 11 B3 Génainville (sic)

name: Unnamed religious complex
description: Referred to today as "Gisacum", this vast Gallo-Roman religious complex (with major spa), is located largely in the French commune of Le Vieil Evreux.

name: Görresburg
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G2 Görresburg

name: Gournay-sur-Aronde
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 C3 Gournay-sur-Aronde

name: Gallo-Roman Sanctuary at Hanches
description: A Gallo-Roman sanctuary identified in the 1960s at La Cavée du Moulin near Hanches in the Eure-et-Loir department of France.

name: Hochscheid
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 H3 Hochscheid

name: Sanctuary of Isis and Magna Mater
description: The Sanctuary of Isis and Magna Mater at Roman Mogontiacum (modern Mainz, Germany) was active between the first century and the third century A.D.

props.ancientName: Sanctuaire d'Isis et de Mater Magna

name: Minatiacum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 E3 Minatiacum

name: Möhn
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G3 Möhn

name: Nanteuil-sur-Aisne
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 E3 Nanteuil-sur-Aisne

name: Tempelbezirk Pesch
description: A Gallo-Roman temple complex dated to the 1st-4th centuries CE.

name: Pelm
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G2 Pelm

name: Pithiviers-le-Vieil
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 C4 Pithiviers-le-Vieil

name: Pommern-Martberg
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 H2 Pommern-Martberg

name: Ribemont-sur-Ancre
description: Ribemont-sur-Ancre is the site of a Celtic and Gallo-Roman sanctuary located on the right bank of the Ancre river, a tributary of the Somme. Roger Agache discovered the site in 1962. The site is vast in its area, covering some 80 ha.

name: Rijsbergen
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 E1 Rijsbergen

name: Rouvroy-les-Merles
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 C3 Rouvroy-les-Merles

name: St-André-sur-Cailly
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 B3 St-André-sur-Cailly

name: St-Aubin-sur-Gaillon
description: St-Aubin-sur-Gaillon was the site of a Gallo-Roman sanctuary.

name: Ste-Ruffine
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G3 Ste-Ruffine

name: Septeuil
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 B4 Septeuil

name: Songeons
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 B3 Songeons

name: Tabernae
description: Remains of a Roman vicus and hilltop temple complex excavated at modern Tawern in Germany.

name: Thalfang-Bäsch
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G3 Thalfang-Bäsch

name: Varnenum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G2 Varnenum

name: Verbinum/Vironum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 D3 Verbinum/Vironum

name: Oberdorla
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 12 D1 Oberdorla

name: Temple of Athena Skiras at Phaleron
description: This sanctuary, reported in Phaleron by Pausanias, is thought to have been located near the modern "old town" of Phaleron in Attica. It is not to be confused with the Temple of Athena Skiras on the island of Salamis, reported by Herodotus.

name: Sanctuary of Hathor (Serabit el-Khadim)
description: Sanctuary of Hathor built in the Middle Kingdom at Serabit el-Khadim.

name: Temple of Augustus et Livia
description: The so-called Temple of Augustus et Livia dates ca. 10 B.C. and was originally dedicated to Roma and Augustus. The temple was located on the ancient forum of the city. By the fifth century the temple had become a Christian church. It was extensively restored in the nineteenth century.

name: Temple of Amphiaraus
description: The Temple of Amphiaraus is an oracular temple dedicated to the hero/god Amphiaraus, located within the sanctuary of the Amphiareion. The site was believed to have healing powers and was particularly important in the Late Classical and Hellenistic periods (fifth to third centuries BCE).

name: Santa Venera al Pozzo
description: An archaeological area in Sicily, near Acireale, possibly related to the ancient settlement of Acium.

name: Etruscan altar of Grotta Porcina
description: A rock-cut altar dating to the first half of the sixth century B.C.

name: Punta della Vipera Etruscan temple
description: A coastal Etruscan sanctuary that dates ca. 540-530 B.C. with additional architectural phases dating to the fourth through second centuries B.C.

name: Ploutonion at Hierapolis
description: The Ploutonion at Hierapolis is a walled sanctuary dedicated to Pluto and Kore.

name: Sanctuary of Athena Chalkioikos
description: Sanctuary of Athena Chalkioikos at the Acropolis of Sparta

name: Flag Fen
description: Flag Fen is a Bronze Age ritual site near Peterborough, England, comprised of 60,000 timbers arranged in five rows that connect Whittlesey Island with Peterborough. Dendrochronological evidence has dates the site to ca. 1365–967 BC.

name: The Deir
description: Monumental religious building carved out of rock in Petra, Jordan which was used for a variety of complex religious ceremonies and customs after the time of its completion in the mid-first century CE.

name: Temple of Heracles
description: The Temple of Heracles is one of the oldest temples in the Valley of the Temples in ancient Akragas, Sicily, dating from around the late 6th century BCE.

name: Temple at es-Snemat
description: An unnamed monument, settlement, or villa of Syrtica

name: An unnamed place
description: An unnamed place of Tripolitana

name: Ara Maxima Herculis
description: An early cult center connected with Herakles located in Rome's Forum Boarium.

name: Shara Temple at Umma
description: Large temple at Tell Jokha/Umma dedicated to the Sumerian god Shara. The temple was constructed by king Šu-Suen of the Third Dynasty of Ur (2035-2027 BCE), and was used during the Ur III-Old Babylonian periods.

name: Sanctuary of Despoina at Lykosoura
description: An ancient sanctuary to Despoina located at Lykosoura, Arcadia.

props.ancientName: Sanctuary of the Mistress at Lykosoura

name: Santalla de Bóveda
description: The Roman sanctuary of Santalla de Bóveda dates to the second through fourth centuries A.D. It is located in the parish of Santalla de Bóveda de Meralocated near Lugo. Discovered in 1926, the site is a protected national monument.

name: Ešiddukišara
description: The temple of the god Nabû, Ešiddukišara (“House of the Director of the Universe”), was located in the Tuba district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of three temples in that part of Babylon.

name: Kidmuri Temple
description: The bīt Kidmuri is one of the temples located in the citadel of Nineveh. Its Sumerian ceremonial name is not preserved in extant cuneiform sources. Although the temple's exact location remains unknown, it is generally believed to have been between the temple of the goddess Ištar (Emašmaš) and the Citadel Gate (East Gate).

name: Le Pinnacle
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 7 D2 Le Pinnacle

name: Gula temple (Kalhu)
description: When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the goddess Gula. Although its location is not known, the Gula temple at Kalhu is thought to have been part of the Ninurta temple complex (in the northwest corner of the citadel), east of the Šarrat-niphi temple and north of the Kidmuri temple.

name: Koh Ker
description: An ancient temple complex of the Khmer Empire, Koh Ker is also known as Lingapura or Chok Gargyar. The site is located in the Srayong commune of Preah, Vihear province's Kulen district. The site was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage site in September 2023 and covers some 1,187 ha.

name: Ekarzagina
description: The temple of the god Ea within the Esagil(a) complex at Babylon. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ekarzagina means "Pure Quay"or "Quay of Lapis Lazuli."

name: Ehulhul
description: The temple of the god Sîn at Harran. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ehulhul means "House which Gives Joy."

name: London Mithraeum
description: A temple sacred to Mithras in Londinium that was discovered in 1954. The visible remains of the temple do not sit on the original site, but have been reassembled at Temple Court, Queen Victoria Street, London.

name: Tchonemyris
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 79 D3 Tchonemyris

name: Touphion
description: Touphion is located on the east bank of the Nile River about 20 km south of Thebes. The ruins of a multi-period temple initially built by Sesostris I were found there.

name: Malchittu nuragic temple
description: A sacred structure with an 'in antis' plan located near Arzachena in Sardinia.

name: Abu Simbel
description: The site of two temples constructed by Ramesses II, one dedicated to the triad of Amun, Ra-Horakhty, and Ptah, as well as the deified Ramesses, and another dedicated to Hathor and the pharaoh's wife Nefertari. The temple was relocated between 1964 and 1968 to prevent its submersion under Lake Nasser after the construction of the Aswan High Dam.

name: Temple of Dionysus Lysios (Thebes)
description: Major cult centre to Dionysus located in Thebes and described by Pausanias as near the Protidian Gate. In mythology, it was the site of the immolation of Semele.

name: Temple of Demeter and Kore
description: A Punic and Roman temple sacred to Demeter and Kore located at Bagoi, Perdaxius, Sardinia.

name: Sebetti temple (Kalhu)
description: When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the divine Sebetti. The location of this building is not known.

name: Red Basilica
description: A Roman temple built above the Selinus river was originally constructed to worship Egyptian gods, most likely Isis and/or Serapis. In the Byzantine era, it was converted into a church of St. John. It was subsequently ruined, although the rotunda on the north side is still an active mosque.

name: Khurha
description: A partially-excavated complex near modern Qum in Iran. The site was possibly a sanctuary and likely dates to the Seleucid age. There is some evidence that the site continued in use into the Parthian period.

name: Enlil temple (Kalhu)
description: When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the god Enlil. Although its location is not known, the Enlil temple at Kalhu is thought to have been situated within the Ninurta temple complex, in the northwest corner of the citadel.

name: Adad-Šala temple (Kalhu)
description: When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the god Adad and the goddess Šala. Although its location is not known, the Adad-Šala temple at Kalhu is thought to have been situated within the Ninurta temple complex, in the northwest corner of the citadel.

name: Sîn temple (Kalhu)
description: When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the moon-god Sîn. Although its location is not known, the Sîn temple at Kalhu is thought to have been situated within the Ninurta temple complex, in the northwest corner of the citadel.

name: Esabad
description: The temple of the goddess Gula, Esabad (“House of the Open Ear”), was located in the Tuba district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of three temples in that part of Babylon. Esabad was rebuilt by the Assyrian king Ashurbanipal (r. 668–ca. 631 BC) and Nebuchadnezzar II (r. 604–652 BC). The temple survived into Arsacid times (247 BC–AD 224).

name: Temple of Divus Augustus (Nola)
description: According to Cassius Dio, Tiberius dedicated a temple to the deified Augustus at a location marking the site of the princeps' death. Its precise location is not known.
